The unique geology creates islands and caves that are revealed at low tide, adding an element of exploration to any visit.

activities.text: Walk the South West Coast Path along the Lizard's dramatic clifftops with views toward Lizard Point

days_plan.itin_title: Cornwall Coastal Road Trip: Castles, Secret Coves & Celtic Heritage Guide

visits.text: Explore the Isle of Portland, the most southerly point of Dorset, a limestone peninsula joined to the mainland by Chesil Beach and steeped in military history. The biggest man-made harbor in the world, of critical importance during both World Wars, speaks to Portland's strategic significance, while today the island offers tranquility, abundant wildlife and dramatic coastal scenery. The distinctive Portland stone quarried here has been used to construct some of Britain's finest buildings, including St Paul's Cathedral.

Visit Portland Bill Lighthouse, the striking red and white tower that has guided ships around this dangerous headland since 1906. The lighthouse offers tours revealing the workings of this vital navigational aid, while the surrounding cliffs provide excellent opportunities for birdwatching and coastal photography. The views from Portland Bill extend across Lyme Bay, with the outline of Devon visible on clear days.

Discover Portland Castle, one of England's best-preserved coastal fortresses from the reign of Henry VIII, built in the 1540s to defend against French and Spanish invasion. This artillery castle, with its distinctive rounded walls designed to deflect cannonballs, tells the story of Tudor England's precarious position in European politics. The castle later served as a private residence and now offers fascinating insights into 500 years of English history.

activities.text: Visit Pulpit Rock, the dramatic coastal feature near the lighthouse, and watch the waves crash against the stone platform

days_plan.itin_title: Dorset's Jurassic Coast Guide: Cliffs, Corfe Castle & Coastal Walks

visits.text: Enter the whimsical world of Portmeirion, an enchanting Italianate village on the North Wales coast that feels like a slice of the Amalfi Coast transported to the shores of Snowdonia. Designed by visionary architect Sir Clough Williams-Ellis between 1925 and 1975, this unique village features colorful buildings, campaniles, and piazzas nestled in subtropical gardens cascading to the Dwyryd Estuary. The pastel-painted facades and Mediterranean architecture create an otherworldly atmosphere unlike anywhere else in Britain.

Wander through Portmeirion's labyrinthine paths discovering hidden courtyards, ornate fountains and architectural salvage from across Europe incorporated into Williams-Ellis's grand design. The village gained worldwide fame as the setting for the cult 1960s television series 'The Prisoner,' and fans still visit to explore the iconic locations. Every corner reveals unexpected details, from classical statues to whimsical follies, creating endless opportunities for exploration.

Explore the extensive woodland gardens surrounding the village, where exotic plants thrive in the mild coastal climate created by the Gulf Stream. The wild gardens, planted with rhododendrons, hydrangeas and rare specimens from around the world, offer miles of scenic coastal walks along the estuary. The views across to Snowdonia's peaks provide a stunning backdrop to this unique architectural fantasy.

activities.text: Take a scenic coastal walk through the woodland gardens with views across the estuary to Snowdonia

days_plan.itin_title: Wales Road Trip: Conwy Castle, Pembrokeshire Coast & Snowdonia

places.name: Portree

visits.text: Stop at Urquhart Castle on the shores of Loch Ness — its spectacular ruins occupy a rocky promontory directly on the water and one of the Grant Tower's five storeys is accessible for a view over the loch. This castle was one of medieval Scotland's largest, and its history reads like a summary of the Wars of Independence: seized and re-seized between Scots and English in the 13th and 14th centuries, then attacked almost continuously by the Macdonald Lords of the Isles until 1545. Finally abandoned, its government troops blew up the gate in 1692 to prevent Jacobites using it. Those collapsed blocks you still see on the ground are from that explosion. Book your slot online: 547,000 people visited in 2019, Scotland's third most-visited site after Edinburgh and Stirling.

Eilean Donan Castle, halfway to Skye on the A87, is Scotland's most photographed castle — and it entirely justifies its reputation. Sitting on an islet at the confluence of three lochs, it was destroyed during the Jacobite wars of 1719 then entirely reconstructed between 1919 and 1932. Stop at least for photos from the bridge: the late-afternoon light on the golden walls with the lochs as backdrop is the image of Scotland.

Before crossing to Skye via the Kyle of Lochalsh bridge, stop at the Five Sisters of Kintail to look back towards the Highlands: five peaks exceeding 900 metres rise like a curtain behind Glen Shiel. It's one of the most striking mountain ranges on the Scottish mainland. You'll find a car park and viewpoint on the A87 — allow 20 minutes for photos.

Arrive in Portree in late afternoon — Skye's only real town, with its harbour ringed by colourful houses. Stroll along the quay to see the fishing boats and eat local seafood. Skye's lobster and crab rank among Scotland's finest.

The Old Man of Storr is Skye's most iconic image: a 55-metre rock column standing alone on a ridge above the Trotternish peninsula, north of Portree. The climb from the car park takes about 45 minutes — the trail is well-marked but heavily trafficked. Leave early morning to avoid tour buses. If mist shrouds the basalt columns, it's even more dramatic. From the top, the view across the Sound of Raasay and the islands below is vertiginous.

The Quiraing, further north on the same Trotternish peninsula, is landscape of another nature. Successive geological landslips have created a labyrinth of cliffs, pinnacles and high grassy ledges. The road skirting this slope from Uig is itself a spectacle — one of Skye's finest scenic drives. The hike into the Quiraing itself demands 2.5 to 3 hours for the full circuit.

The Fairy Glen near Uig is a small valley with spiralling grassy mounds that geology has sculpted into a limestone hollow — it's easy to see why locals associated these rounded forms with fairy rings. It's small, quiet and otherworldly. Allow 30 to 45 minutes for a circular walk around the mounds.

Kilt Rock on Trotternish's east coast merits a 20-minute stop: a basalt cliff of hexagonal columns plunging straight into the sea, with a waterfall leaping off the edge from the plateau. The resemblance to pleated kilt fabric is striking. There's a secure clifftop viewpoint — the views across the sea and jagged coastline are impressive.

The Fairy Pools of Glenbrittle are Skye's most popular hike — and they deserve their reputation. A series of translucent turquoise pools fed by waterfalls descends at the foot of the black Cuillin range. The water colour is real: reflections from sky and rocks create this blue-green even under clouds. The trail from the car park is easy, 2.4 km to the first pools. In 1601, these pools were the scene of a bloody battle between the Macdonalds of Sleat and the Macleods of Dunvegan — the waters supposedly ran red for days. Arrive as early as possible: tour buses arrive from 10am. Car park is paid but access to the pools is free.

Stop at the old Sligachan Bridge returning to Portree — this 19th-century stone bridge over the River Sligachan is one of the classic viewpoints of the Cuillin. Local legend says dipping your face in the river water for 7 seconds grants eternal beauty. The Sligachan Inn, open since 1830, faces the bridge and serves local whiskies.

activities.text: A cruise on Loch Ness from Clansman Harbour to Urquhart Castle is a different way to discover the site — you'll approach the ruins from the water, with the view of the Highlands unfolding behind. The cruise ticket includes castle entry. Operators run these cruises from Drumnadrochit. Book ahead in high season.

Horse riding across Skye's moorland from the Skye Riding Centre — a different experience to see the island from the saddle rather than on foot. The centre offers rides for all levels across open landscapes of the peninsula. Book in advance.

If you have energy and solid hiking boots, the walk to Talisker Bay on Skye's west coast rewards the effort: a black pebble beach at the foot of spectacular cliffs, with volcanic rocks sculpted by the sea and, on clear days, views to the Outer Hebrides. Allow 3 hours round trip from the Carbost car park. In the evening, stop at Talisker Distillery to sample a peaty whisky — it's Skye's only distillery.

visits.text: Regent's Park was designed by John Nash from 1811 commissioned by the Prince Regent (future George IV), who wanted a royal residence surrounded by gardens. The residence never materialized, but the 166 hectares of parkland remained. Queen Mary's Gardens contain one of England's largest rose gardens with over 12,000 roses of 85 varieties - at their peak in June. The Open Air Theatre, active since 1932, presents Shakespeare plays and musicals from April to September.

activities.text: Climb Primrose Hill, the hill north of the park that peaks at 78 metres - it's one of the best free panoramas over London's skyline, with the Shard, London Eye and City clearly identifiable. The spot is particularly popular with Londoners in the evening and on weekends.

London Zoo is on the park's northern edge since 1828 - it's the world's oldest scientific zoo still in operation. Allow half a day if you want to see it all. The Gorilla Kingdom, designed by architect Berthold Lubetkin in the 1930s, is listed for its modernist architecture.

days_plan.itin_title: What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

visits.text: Three Cliffs Bay is often cited as the most beautiful beach in Wales. Three limestone arches plunge straight into the sea at the end of a long sandy beach crossed by an estuary you must ford at low tide—which adds to the end-of-the-world feel. No car access to the beach: allow 25 minutes on foot from the Southgate or Pennard car park. The effort is amply rewarded.

On the ridge overlooking Three Cliffs Bay, the ruins of Pennard Castle emerge from the dunes. Built by the Normans in the 12th century to control the coastline, it has been gradually invaded by sand since the 14th century—local legend says it was buried as divine punishment. Access from the coastal path is immediate and the view from the ruined walls over the bay is striking.

On the way to Rhossili, Oxwich Bay deserves a stop: a wide 3 km beach facing south, sheltered by a pine forest and classified as a National Nature Reserve. The Oxwich Burrows dunes behind the beach are one of Gower's largest dune systems, home to rare plant species.

activities.text: The coastal loop from Southgate around Three Cliffs Bay and Pennard Burrows is one of the best walks in Gower: 8 km, little elevation, scenery that changes every ten minutes between cliffs, dunes, estuaries and beaches. Pass behind Pennard Castle for the plunging view over Three Cliffs from the rear.

days_plan.itin_title: South Wales Circuit: Mountains and Coast

visits.text: Did you know that Richmond in Virginia, Richmond in Australia and Richmond-upon-Thames in London all owe their names to this small Yorkshire town? Henry Tudor had inherited the title Earl of Richmond; becoming Henry VII, he baptised his palace at Sheen with the name of his Yorkshire property - and the London district has borne that name ever since. Climb to Richmond Castle to understand why: perched on a cliff above the Swale, built by Alan the Red after the Norman Conquest around 1071, it possesses more 11th-century Norman architecture than any other castle in England. Local legend claims King Arthur and his knights sleep beneath the foundations - the English love their legends.

Don't miss the Georgian Theatre Royal just below the castle - it's Britain's most intact Georgian theatre, founded in 1788, closed in 1848 and used as a warehouse for a century before being restored and reopened in 1963. Its 200-seat auditorium with original painted decor is tiny and fascinating. If there's a performance during your visit, it's a rare experience not to be missed.

Take the road west from Richmond and you enter Swaledale - the wildest of the Yorkshire Dales. Dry-stone walls divide pastures to infinity, 18th-century hay barns punctuate the hills, villages like Reeth and Muker seem unchanged for two centuries. Drive to Keld at the valley's end for the Kisdon Force waterfalls - it's the road's terminus and everything beyond is on foot.

activities.text: Hiking in Swaledale to the hidden waterfalls around Keld: several short trails (2-4km) lead to Kisdon Force and Catrake Force from the village. The landscape of moorland and dry-stone walls is different from anything you've seen in the Lake District.

The Wensleydale Creamery at Hawes, heading up the valley of the same name from Richmond, offers creamery tours with tasting. Wensleydale is a crumbly, slightly honeyed cheese, produced in this valley since Cistercian monks from Roquefort settled here in 1156 and brought their maturing techniques. Hawes is about 45km from Richmond.

days_plan.itin_title: Lake District and North-East Loop: Lakes, Dales and Cathedrals
Yorkshire and North East England: Dales, Castles and Coastal Drama

visits.text: The Royal Academy of Arts has occupied Burlington House on Piccadilly since 1868. Founded in 1768 with Joshua Reynolds as first president, it's the UK's oldest institution dedicated to the fine arts. Its temporary exhibitions are among London's most sought-after—the annual Summer Exhibition, running since 1769 without interruption, is the world's longest-running exhibition series. Auguste Rodin exhibited here in 1914 during his first major exhibition in his lifetime, before bequeathing his sculptures to the institution as a symbol of Franco-British friendship. Check the programme before visiting: the permanent collections are less spectacular than the major temporary exhibitions.

activities.text: Check the current exhibition programme on the Royal Academy website before planning your visit—some blockbuster exhibitions sell out weeks in advance.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries

visits.text: Rye is arguably the best-preserved medieval town in southeast England. Perched on a hill that was once an island surrounded by marshes, it was part of the Cinque Ports—the confederation of ports that supplied ships to the Crown in exchange for trading privileges. Start with Mermaid Street: this isn't a reconstruction, it's one of England's oldest streets, with timber-framed houses dating back to the 12th century. The Mermaid Inn was rebuilt in 1420 after a French raid; in the 18th century, the Hawkhurst Gang, a terrifying band of smugglers, made it their headquarters. Legend has it that one evening, twenty gang members fired shots in the neighboring street for fun, and a too-curious passerby, James Marshall, disappeared without a trace.

Ypres Tower, built in 1249 to defend the town from French attacks, is Rye's oldest monument. It served successively as a fortress, private residence, courthouse, prison, and morgue before becoming a museum. Climb to the top for views over the Rother estuary and Romney Marsh in the distance. Don't miss the prison cells and the medieval herb garden below. The Women's Tower in the same complex was, in 1857, the country's first women-only prison.

Lamb House, on Church Square, is one of Kent's most intimate literary addresses. Henry James lived here from 1898 to 1916 and wrote several of his major novels here, including The Wings of the Dove. E.F. Benson succeeded him and transformed Rye into Tilling, the setting for his comic series Mapp and Lucia. The Georgian house and its walled garden now belong to the National Trust. Allow 45 minutes for the visit.

Finish your tour at St Mary's Church, whose history goes back 900 years. The chiming clock installed in 1561 is the oldest church clock with original mechanism still operating in the UK. Climb the bell tower—entry is paid but modest—for panoramic views over Rye's rooftops, the marshes, and, on clear days, out to sea.

activities.text: Get lost in Rye's twittens—those narrow Sussex alleyways that connect streets. They were much favored by smugglers in the 18th century for slipping between houses unseen. Rye is also known for its antique dealers; if you enjoy browsing, the Strand and its surroundings have several shops worth checking out. If you'd like to pick up a good bottle for the next leg, Gusbourne and Oxney Organic Estate vineyards are about 20 minutes' drive away.

In the evening, settle in at the Ypres Castle Inn, a 17th-century pub nestled in the tower gardens, with a lovely walled garden in summer and a stone fireplace in winter. The place is frequented by locals—you won't find the touristy pub atmosphere of the town center.

days_plan.itin_title: Southeast England & Kent Circuit: White Cliffs and Secret Gardens

visits.text: Departure from London to the Southwest.
Stonehenge and its megalithic circle. (UNESCO).
Salisbury Cathedral, England's highest spire.

activities.text: Guided tour of Stonehenge with audio guide.
Discovery of the Magna Carta in Salisbury.

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest England: Dramatic Cliffs and Coastal Village Charm

places.name: Saltburn By The

visits.text: Sandsend, 5 kilometres north of Whitby, is a sand-and-shingle beach on the Jurassic coast—one of the richest on the English coast for marine fossils. Ammonites, belemnites and marine reptile teeth from the Upper Jurassic regularly emerge from the cliff after storms. No special equipment needed: the shoreline at low tide is enough. The beach is uncrowded outside summer.

Staithes, wedged between two headlands (Cowbar Nab and Penny Nab) at the mouth of a ravine, is one of the most authentic fishing villages on the north coast. Its lanes are so narrow—the narrowest, Dog Loup, is 45 cm wide—that the sun only enters at midday. Leave your car at the clifftop car park and walk down. In 1745, the young James Cook, aged 16, came here to work as a grocer's apprentice under William Sanderson. He didn't last 18 months: fascinated by the boats in the harbour, he left for Whitby to join the merchant navy. Sanderson's shop was destroyed by a storm but its materials were reused to build a cottage you'll still find on Church Street.

The Captain Cook and Staithes Heritage Centre, in the former 1880 Methodist chapel at the top of the village, holds over 200 books, 61 original engravings from Cook's voyages and dozens of objects related to his life. The upper floor recreates William Sanderson's shop as it was in 1745—the only such recreation in the world. Free entry, donations welcome.

The Saltburn Cliff Tramway is the oldest water-balanced cliff lift still operating in the UK. Opened in June 1884, it connects the Victorian town atop the cliff to the pier and beach 37 metres below, on 63 metres of track at a 71% gradient. Its operating principle has barely changed in 140 years: a 1,100-litre water tank is filled in the upper car until its weight exceeds that of the lower car—the journey from one level to the other takes 55 seconds, controlled by a brakesman in person. Allow 15 minutes for the round trip.

Saltburn's Victorian pier, opened in May 1869 and Grade II* listed, is the only major pleasure pier surviving on the entire North-East coast of England. It extends 200 metres over the North Sea. Walk to the end to see the coast recede in both directions—on clear days, the Boulby cliffs to the north (England's highest, at 203 metres) are visible.

Saltburn-by-the-Sea beach, a long strip of sand and shingle at the foot of the cliffs, is a recognised surf spot on the north coast. Beyond the water, the Victorian seafront—colourful bathing huts, esplanades, valley gardens in the background—is intact and particularly photogenic at sunset when the light takes on an orange tint.

activities.text: Fossil hunting on Sandsend beach and cliffs: armed with a sharp eye and patience, you'll find ammonites on the surface among the pebbles after storms. Operators like Yorkshire Coast Rock run guided outings on this coastal stretch if you want to stack the odds—book ahead, groups are small.

Walk down into Staithes to the harbour: allow 15 minutes downhill (very steep for the last 200 metres) and 20 minutes back up. At the bottom, the quay is tiny, the cobles (small flat-bottomed boats) are often hauled onto the bank. Take time to follow Staithes Beck to the sea. Don't attempt to drive down—the lanes are too narrow for any non-resident vehicle.

Surfing or stand-up paddleboarding lesson on Saltburn beach: hire operators work on the main beach in season. The Saltburn surf break is one of the most reputed in northern England—consistent swell, sandy bottom, cold water but viable in a wetsuit. If you'd rather watch, stay atop the cliff and watch the surfers battle the tide.

Ride the Saltburn Cliff Tramway: take the car both ways if you want to see the mechanism at work, and watch the brakesman operate at the top. The view over the beach and pier from the tracks, mid-slope, is one of the finest of the leg.

days_plan.itin_title: North York Moors Coast Road Trip: Cliffs and Fishing Villages
Yorkshire: Wild Coast & Secret Moorlands
Yorkshire: Wild Coast & Secret Moorlands

map_keywords: Captain Cook and Staithes Heritage Centre

visits.text: Sandringham Estate has been the British royal family's private residence since 1862, when Queen Victoria purchased it for her eldest son, the future Edward VII, for £220,000. The current red-brick Jacobean-style house was built in 1870 by architect A.J. Humbert. George V wrote of Sandringham: 'the place I love better than anywhere in the world'. George VI died here in 1952. Eight ground-floor rooms, virtually unchanged since Edwardian times, open to the public from late March to early October. The 24-hectare Country Park is open year-round for free.

St Mary Magdalene church, on the Sandringham estate, is the royal family's parish church—where the Christmas service watched by Britons on television each year is held. Built in the 14th century and extensively remodelled by Edward VII, it houses several royal memorials. Don't miss the Silver Jubilee Gate in solid silver, a gift from the royal family in 1977.

activities.text: After the house and church, wander freely in Sandringham Country Park—24 hectares of woods and heathland open year-round. In season, the estate's organic farm sells its own produce at the shop. It's a good moment to catch your breath before the final day.

days_plan.itin_title: East Anglia Circuit: Cambridge, Suffolk Coast and Royal Norfolk

visits.text: The RSPB reserve at Bempton Cliffs, about 30 kilometres south of Scarborough, is one of England's great wildlife experiences. The chalk cliffs rise 120 metres above the North Sea, and between April and July they host over 200,000 nesting seabirds—the only mainland gannet colony in all of Great Britain (more than 11,000 pairs), plus puffins, guillemots, razorbills and kittiwakes. Gannets have a 2-metre wingspan and sometimes glide eye-level past the viewing platforms. Bring binoculars—puffins blend in among the guillemots. Best period: May to July.

Flamborough Head, the chalk promontory jutting into the North Sea, has two lighthouses: the old one (1674, in white chalk, England's oldest intact lighthouse) and the new one (1806, still in service). From the coastal paths around the headland, you look straight down sheer cliffs to sea caves below—keep an eye out for seals sometimes basking on the rocks.

Scarborough Castle dominates a 91-metre-high rocky headland with cliffs on three sides, between the town's two bays. The site has been occupied for 3,000 years: Roman signal station in the 4th century, Anglo-Saxon chapel, then royal fortress from 1159 when Henry II seized it and built the great stone keep. It was here that Edward II's favourite, Piers Gaveston, took refuge in 1312 before being captured. In December 1914, German naval bombardment (SMS Derfflinger and SMS Von der Tann) damaged the keep—the shell impacts are still visible. Managed by English Heritage, it's a vast 16-acre site with panoramic views over both bays. Allow 1h30.

South Bay, the great sandy beach sheltered below the castle, is Scarborough's historic beach—the town claims to be England's first seaside resort, having attracted spa visitors to its mineral springs as early as the 1620s. In season, the colourful bathing huts and the Victorian Spa are an almost intact period backdrop. For you, the essentials: a seaside walk and an ice cream.

The Scarborough Maritime Heritage Centre, near the old harbour, traces the town's history of deep-sea fishing and maritime trade. Collections of model boats, fishing gear and fishermen's family testimonies. Small but well documented, the place is often quiet—an hour suffices.

activities.text: The coastal path around the castle headland links South Bay to North Bay via the ramparts—a 45-minute walk with plunging views over both bays and the fishing harbour below. Start from the castle entrance and descend to the old harbour on the far side to see the fishing boats.

North Bay, quieter and less touristy than South Bay, is Scarborough's surf spot. If you don't have a board, local operators offer lessons and rentals on the beach in season. The water's cold even in summer—wetsuit essential.

days_plan.itin_title: North York Moors Coast Road Trip: Cliffs and Fishing Villages

visits.text: The Science Museum in South Kensington holds over 300,000 objects in its collections and houses one of the most striking pieces in all of London: the Apollo 10 capsule nicknamed Charlie Brown, the only Apollo spacecraft displayed outside the United States. In May 1969, Thomas Stafford, John Young and Eugene Cernan conducted a dress rehearsal for the first Moon landing in this capsule, approaching within 14.5 km of the lunar surface without touching down. The capsule arrived at the Science Museum in 1976 and has resided in the new Space gallery inaugurated in 2025, alongside the Soyuz TMA-19M capsule that brought British astronaut Tim Peake back to Earth. You can still see the hand-scribbled calculations by astronauts on the interior walls. Nearby in the Making the Modern World gallery, don't miss Stephenson's Rocket from 1829 and the first commercial steam engine.

activities.text: If you're visiting with children under 14, the Wonderlab is an interactive space dedicated to scientific experiments—plan for a separate ticket and book ahead.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries

visits.text: Ironbridge Gorge has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1986—and the title 'birthplace of the Industrial Revolution' is no exaggeration. This is where Abraham Darby I, in 1709, developed the technique of smelting iron with coke, making metal production infinitely cheaper and faster. His grandson, Abraham Darby III, erected the world's first iron bridge across the Severn in 1779: 30 metres span, 378 tonnes of cast iron from the Coalbrookdale foundries. The first tolls on this bridge weren't collected until 1 January 1781. You can still cross the bridge on foot—it's been closed to traffic since 1934.

The ten museums of the Ironbridge Gorge site cover the entire industrial history of the valley—you needn't do them all, but three deserve your time. Start with the Coalbrookdale Museum of Iron, housed in the old blast furnaces: this is where it all began in 1709. Then push on to Blists Hill Victorian Town, a full-scale recreation of an 1900 industrial town with working shops and costumed actors—allow at least 2 hours. If you have a soft spot for decorative arts, the Jackfield Tile Museum is worth the detour: the faience tiles produced here once adorned London Underground stations and palaces worldwide. A combined Passport ticket covers all ten sites with no time limit.

Shrewsbury, 30km northwest of Ironbridge, is a near-intact medieval town nestled in a meander of the Severn—the river encircles the old town on three sides. The historic centre contains more than 660 timber-framed buildings dating from the 15th and 16th centuries. The Old Market Hall from 1596 still presides over the main square. Wander the 'shuts'—covered passageways between houses dating from the Middle Ages—to lose yourself in the lower town.

Shrewsbury Castle, in red sandstone, has overlooked the river since the 11th century. It now houses a military museum, but the view across the medieval rooftops of the old town from the castle gardens is worth the visit. The Quarry park, on the banks of the Severn, contains 'the Dingle'—a sunken garden maintained since the 19th century, one of England's most floral.

activities.text: The Severn at Ironbridge is a working and accessible river. Local operators offer kayak or canoe trips from the banks below the Iron Bridge—an original way to see the gorge from the water, where barges once carried cast iron downriver to Bristol. Allow at least 2 hours.
Local companies run boat trips on the Severn from Shrewsbury, passing under the old stone bridges and along the wooded banks of the meander. Some evening cruises include a meal on board.

days_plan.itin_title: Midlands and Peak District Road Trip: Heritage and Wild Landscapes
The Midlands and Peak District Essentials

places.name: Sir John Soanes Museum

visits.text: Sir John Soane's Museum at Lincoln's Inn Fields is one of London's strangest and most fascinating places—and it's free. John Soane (1753–1837), architect of the Bank of England, transformed three adjoining townhouses into a labyrinth of rooms where he crammed his collections according to personal logic: Egyptian sarcophagi, Roman columns, Hogarth paintings, architectural models, Greco-Roman antiquities. The house is preserved exactly as it was at his death in 1837, by an Act of Parliament he had passed during his lifetime. The Picture Room is a marvel of ingenuity: Soane installed hinged panels that unfold to reveal 118 paintings hidden behind each other—including Hogarth's eight-painting series A Rake's Progress (1733). In the Sepulchral Chamber rests the alabaster sarcophagus of Pharaoh Seti I, discovered in Egypt in 1812 and considered one of the most important objects ever found in Egypt. Arrive early—the museum is very popular and space is limited.

activities.text: The Picture Room panels are opened at fixed times (11am, 2pm, 3pm, 4pm)—plan your visit to witness the opening of the panels, it's a special moment.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries

map_keywords: Sir John Soane Museum Lincoln's Inn Fields London

visits.text: Aysgarth Falls in Wensleydale is the Thames in Yorkshire version: the River Ure tumbles over three levels of limestone slabs through an oak forest. Robin Hood: Prince of Thieves (1991) was filmed here. Make the detour to the Lower Falls - less frequented than the Upper and considerably more impressive after rain, which in England isn't a rare problem.

Skipton Castle deserves more than a glance from the street. Built from 1090, remodelled in the 14th century, it withstood a three-year siege during the Civil War (1642-1645) before being partially dismantled on Cromwell's orders. Lady Anne Clifford restored it in the 1650s - the yew tree she planted in the inner courtyard is still there, three and a half centuries later. Climb onto the ramparts for views over the town and canal. It's one of England's best-preserved medieval castles and tickets remain reasonable.

Make the detour to Bolton Abbey, 8km east of Skipton on the banks of the Wharfe - one of England's most beautiful monastic ruins, and entry is free for pedestrians. The 12th-century Augustinian ruins sit alongside a priory still in service for Sunday worship, creating a strange and endearing mix. The estate belongs to the Duke of Devonshire. The 5km walk around the river's meander is easy and magnificent - allow 1h30.

activities.text: Skipton market is one of Yorkshire's oldest and liveliest, held since 1204. It runs Monday, Wednesday, Friday and Saturday on the High Street. If you pass on one of those days, you'll find local cheeses, charcuterie, artisan breads and produce from Dales farms.

The walk along Skipton's ramparts follows the Leeds-Liverpool Canal - Britain's longest single-lock canal (204km, completed 1816). A 3km round trip from the castle gives you a sense of the peaceful atmosphere of Yorkshire's canals.

days_plan.itin_title: Lake District and North-East Loop: Lakes, Dales and Cathedrals

visits.text: Immerse yourself in the breathtaking landscapes of Snowdonia National Park, home to Wales' highest peaks and most dramatic mountain scenery. The park encompasses 823 square miles of rugged mountains, glacial lakes, ancient forests and wild moorland that has inspired artists, poets and adventurers for centuries. On clear days, the views across the peaks create some of the most spectacular panoramas in Britain.

Challenge yourself on Mount Snowdon, the highest mountain in Wales at 1,085 meters, whose summit can be reached by several hiking trails of varying difficulty or by the famous Snowdon Mountain Railway. The Pyg Track and Miners' Track are popular routes offering stunning views over Glaslyn and Llyn Llydaw, glacial lakes that shimmer like jewels in their mountain bowls. On the summit, on clear days, views extend to Ireland, Scotland and England.

Discover the park's hidden valleys, cascading waterfalls and ancient oak woodlands that create a landscape of endless variety and beauty. Historic slate quarries, their terraces carved into mountainsides, tell the story of the industry that shaped North Wales and exported Welsh slate across the world. The mountain villages, with their stone cottages and Welsh-speaking communities, maintain traditions stretching back centuries.

activities.text: Take the Snowdon Mountain Railway from Llanberis for a scenic ascent to the summit without the hiking effort

days_plan.itin_title: Wales Road Trip: Conwy Castle, Pembrokeshire Coast & Snowdonia

visits.text: Soho developed from the 17th century as the neighbourhood of Huguenot refugees fleeing France after the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes in 1685. This tradition of welcoming marginalized communities persists: Soho successively became the Victorian artistic bohemia neighbourhood, of jazz in the 1950s, of rock in the 1960s, then the heart of Britain's LGBTQ+ community. Old Compton Street has been its living centre since the 1970s. In the evening, Wardour Street and its surroundings concentrate an exceptional density of bars, restaurants and concert venues for such a small area.

activities.text: The West End begins east of Soho - Shaftesbury Avenue alone concentrates six theatres. The TKTS booth on Leicester Square sells discounted tickets for evening performances on the day - turn up in early afternoon to have choice. London theatres often offer 'standing tickets' sold an hour before the show for a fraction of the price.

For dinner, Soho offers one of London's most diverse restaurant offerings in such a compact area - Vietnamese cuisine on Wardour Street, Japanese izakayas on Brewer Street, trattorias on Dean Street. Frith Street houses one of London's oldest jazz clubs still active, open since the 1950s - check the programme before you go.

days_plan.itin_title: What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

visits.text: Don't miss Sutton Hoo en route to Southwold—one of Europe's most important archaeological sites. In June 1939, self-taught archaeologist Basil Brown uncovered beneath a burial mound a 27-metre-long ship and a burial chamber filled with early 7th-century Anglo-Saxon treasures: 37 solid gold coins (Frankish tremisses), 5th-century Byzantine silverware, garnets from Sri Lanka and a human-faced helmet that became the symbol of the Anglo-Saxon period. The deceased is probably King Rædwald of East Anglia, who died around 625. The originals are in the British Museum, but the National Trust-managed site offers an excellent exhibition and visits to the mounds. Allow 1.5 hours.

Framlingham Castle is one of England's best-preserved medieval castles, with a wall-walk on the battlements offering clear views across the Suffolk countryside. Built between 1190 and 1210 by Roger Bigod, it's famous as the place where Mary Tudor awaited the battle result that would carry her to the English throne in 1553. The town of Framlingham invites a stroll: its market, pubs and quiet atmosphere are typically Suffolk.

Southwold Pier, built in 1900 to welcome steamships from London, has been rebuilt several times after storms. Its current 623-foot version dates from 2001. Enter the Under the Pier Show: a collection of deliberately absurd slot machines, the work of artist Tim Hunkin, where you can notably 'sell modern art with a machine gun'. The pier is free to access, games cost extra.

Southwold's multicoloured beach huts are the town's postcard image—there are 266, all privately owned. Originally they were Victorian 'bathing machines' on wheels allowing ladies to change unseen. The golden sand beach is one of Suffolk's finest. Heading back to the centre, explore the lanes around Market Place—independent shops, pubs, and the local brewery producing beers since 1872.

Walberswick, on the other bank of the Blyth, is a peaceful fishing village with a long sandy beach and RSPB-managed bird marshes. Much quieter than Southwold, it's a good counterpoint for anyone seeking solitude.

Orford Castle is one of England's most fascinating castles, with its 21-sided polygonal keep—unique in the country. Henry II had it built between 1165 and 1173 to counter the power of Suffolk barons, notably Hugh Bigod of Framlingham. Construction cost £1,413 sterling, a colossal sum at the time, and detailed accounts are preserved in the royal Pipe Rolls. Climb to the roof for views over Orford Ness. Inside, read the legend of the Wildman of Orford: in 1167, fishermen supposedly caught in their nets a wild man covered in hair, held six months in the castle without ever speaking a word, before escaping to the sea.

Aldeburgh is a former Tudor port town turned discreet and elegant seaside resort, intimately linked to composer Benjamin Britten who founded the Aldeburgh Festival here in 1948—still one of Britain's major classical music festivals. On the shingle beach, the 16th-century Moot Hall now sits at the water's edge, after coastal erosion swallowed everything around it. Fishermen's huts sell smoked fish and crabs directly on the beach.

Thorpeness, 2km north of Aldeburgh, is an entirely artificial village designed from 1910 by eccentric Stuart Ogilvie: a neo-Tudor holiday community with a 65-acre artificial lake (the Meare). The 'House in the Clouds' perched at 21 metres actually concealed a 50,000-gallon water tower—decorated on the facade to look like an ordinary house so as not to spoil the village aesthetic. Impossible not to smile seeing this.

activities.text: Arriving in Southwold late afternoon, take an initial tour of the seafront. The white 31-metre lighthouse, built in 1887 right in the town centre, is open for visits—113 steps for a panoramic coastal view. On Gun Hill, six 18th-century cannons face the sea: they commemorate the 1672 Battle of Sole Bay where the Anglo-French and Dutch fleets clashed right here. An excellent appetizer for the rest of your stay.

The small rowing ferry crossing between Southwold and Walberswick is one of the little joys of this coast. The service is seasonal (generally April to October)—otherwise take the bridge on foot, 20 minutes. On the other side, walk along the beach to the RSPB marshes for birdwatching, then return via the dunes. Allow 2 hours round trip.
Small pedestrian ferry crossing between Southwold and Walberswick

Walk on Southwold Pier, built in 1900 as a landing stage for steamers bringing tourists from London. Dynamited during World War II to prevent a German landing, struck by a drifting mine in 1941, hit by storms in 1955, it was completely rebuilt and now extends 190 metres. It houses a collection of eccentric coin-operated machines created by inventor Tim Hunkin.
Visit the local brewery, established since 1872 on East Green, on a site where beer has been brewed for at least 670 years. Tasting of craft beers and spirits distilled on site.

days_plan.itin_title: East Anglia Circuit: Cambridge, Suffolk Coast and Royal Norfolk
East Anglia Circuit: Cambridge, Suffolk Coast and Royal Norfolk
East Anglia Circuit: Cambridge, Suffolk Coast and Royal Norfolk
East Anglia Road Trip: Norfolk, Suffolk and Cambridge

visits.text: Marvel at the Eden Project, one of Cornwall's most innovative attractions, where enormous biodome structures house botanical gardens showcasing plant species from around the world. Since opening in 2001, this futuristic complex has captivated visitors with its striking exhibitions, interactive experiences and passionate commitment to environmental education. The massive biomes, built in a reclaimed clay pit, create tropical rainforest and Mediterranean climates under their transparent canopies.

Explore St Austell, an old market town that played an essential role in Cornwall's mining and China Clay industries that once made this region prosperous. Reminders of the town's historic importance can still be spotted throughout the streets, including the bygone Cornwall Coliseum. The St Austell Brewing Company offers tours and tastings of traditional Cornish ales brewed using centuries-old methods.

Wander through the Lost Gardens of Heligan, a hidden gem covering more than 200 acres of restored Victorian gardens and woodlands near Mevagissey. Suspended bridges, moss-covered statues and jungle-like plantings create an atmosphere of rediscovered wonder in these gardens that lay abandoned and overgrown for decades until their restoration began in the 1990s. The story of the gardens' neglect during World War I and subsequent rescue adds poignant historical depth to their natural beauty.

activities.text: Spend a half-day exploring the Eden Project's biomes and outdoor gardens

days_plan.itin_title: Cornwall Coastal Road Trip: Castles, Secret Coves & Celtic Heritage Guide

visits.text: Marvel at the Green Bridge of Wales, a spectacular natural limestone arch dramatically jutting into the sea on the Pembrokeshire coast. This remarkable geological formation, carved by millennia of wave action, creates one of Wales' most photographed natural wonders. The nearby Elegug Stacks, two giant pillars of stone rising from the sea, add to this dramatic coastal landscape that showcases the raw power of nature.

Explore St Davids, Britain's smallest city and the most westerly point of Wales, built around its magnificent medieval cathedral. St Davids Cathedral, nestled in a peaceful hollow, is the final resting place of Wales' patron saint and has been a site of pilgrimage for over 1,500 years. The purple-hued stone building with its distinctive square tower represents one of the finest examples of medieval religious architecture in Britain.

Watch a spectacular sunset at Whitesands Beach near St Davids, one of the most stunning coastal experiences in Wales. This beautiful Blue Flag beach offers pristine golden sands backed by dramatic headlands, with views stretching across St Brides Bay. The sunsets here paint the sky in extraordinary colors, creating magical moments that visitors remember long after leaving.

activities.text: Book a boat tour with Thousand Island Expeditions to Skomer Island, home to the largest Atlantic puffin colony in southern Britain

days_plan.itin_title: Wales Road Trip: Conwy Castle, Pembrokeshire Coast & Snowdonia

visits.text: Discover St Ives, perhaps the most beloved destination in Cornwall, where expansive golden beaches stretch beneath a picturesque town of cobbled streets and whitewashed cottages. For centuries, this fishing village has attracted visitors drawn by its exceptional light, beautiful beaches and artistic heritage. The combination of seaside charm, cultural attractions and natural beauty makes St Ives irresistible to first-time visitors and returning devotees alike.

Visit Tate St Ives, the modern art gallery that showcases works by artists who have been inspired by Cornwall's extraordinary light and landscape since the 1920s. The gallery's striking architecture, perched above Porthmeor Beach, provides the perfect setting for collections that include works by Barbara Hepworth, Ben Nicholson and other luminaries of the St Ives School. The views from the gallery windows compete with the art on the walls for visitors' attention.

Explore the winding cobbled streets connecting cute corners, seaside pubs and traditional pasty shops that give St Ives its irresistible character. The harbor, still home to a working fishing fleet, provides a picturesque heart to the town, while the beaches on either side offer different characters for swimming, surfing or simply sunbathing. Boat trips depart regularly to spot dolphins or see the iconic Godrevy Lighthouse up close.

Relax on the beautiful beaches of St Ives, which are particularly impressive at low tide when vast expanses of golden sand are revealed. Porthmeor Beach attracts surfers and art lovers drawn to the nearby Tate, while the sheltered Harbour Beach offers calmer waters for families. The town's position on a narrow peninsula means you're never far from the sound of the waves, wherever you wander.

activities.text: Take a boat trip from the harbor to spot dolphins and see Godrevy Lighthouse, inspiration for Virginia Woolf's novel

days_plan.itin_title: Cornwall Coastal Road Trip: Castles, Secret Coves & Celtic Heritage Guide
Southwest England: Dramatic Cliffs and Coastal Village Charm
England Grand Tour: From Cornwall to the Northern Lakes

visits.text: St Katharine Docks is the last major historic dock still visible near central London. Opened in 1828 after the demolition of an entire neighbourhood (11,000 people displaced), it was intended for precious goods - ivory, sugar, coffee, wool. Closed in 1968 for failing to adapt to containers, it was converted into a pleasure marina. Today, luxury yachts sit alongside historic barges in a basin metres from the Tower of London. The atmosphere is calm and surprising for anyone arriving from the tourist chaos of Tower Bridge.

activities.text: Stroll along the pontoons and observe the sailboats and barges moored - some are lived in year-round. The restaurant and bar terraces around the dock are pleasant in late afternoon. From St Katharine Docks, you can walk to Wapping High Street in 10 minutes to see the old warehouses converted into flats - one of the most authentic Docklands neighbourhoods.

days_plan.itin_title: What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

places.name: Stornoway

visits.text: The CalMac ferry from Ullapool drops you into Stornoway after a 2 hour 45 minute crossing across the Minch - and that crossing is the beginning, not just the journey. The name Stornoway comes from the Old Norse Stjórnavágr, meaning 'steering bay', which tells you something about how long people have been navigating these waters. The town is the capital and only real urban centre of the Outer Hebrides, population just over 8,000, and it carries that role with quiet confidence. Walk the harbour quay first - fishing boats still land their catch here every morning, and the smell of peat drifts across the water from the smokehouses that cure the seafood before it reaches the island's restaurants.

Lews Castle, the Victorian neo-Gothic pile set in woodland above the harbour, was built in the 1840s for Sir James Matheson, who bought the entire island in 1844 on the back of a fortune made in the opium trade. In 2016, after a £19.5 million restoration, it reopened as Museum nan Eilean - and it's worth every minute inside. Six of the Lewis Chessmen are displayed here: those extraordinary 12th-century carved walrus ivory pieces found in a sandbank at Uig on the west coast of Lewis in 1831, probably made in Norway and lost or hidden here, nobody knows why. The British Museum has 82 of them; this is your chance to see some up close without going to London.

On the way out of Stornoway, the Lewis War Memorial on Cnoc nan Uan hill - a 85-foot Scottish Baronial tower built in 1924 - is visible from half the roads approaching town. It commemorates the island's dead from the First World War, and the number of names on those walls is devastating for a community this size. But the memorial also stands near the site of the Iolaire disaster of 1919: a naval yacht carrying 280 returning Lewis servicemen home from the war struck rocks in Stornoway harbour on New Year's Day and sank with 201 men drowned - within sight of their families waiting on the pier. It remains one of the worst peacetime maritime disasters in British waters.

Drive the 28 km to Callanish (Calanais) on the west coast - and give yourself the whole morning. The Callanish Standing Stones were erected around 2750 BC, pre-dating Stonehenge by several centuries, and unlike Stonehenge there are no barriers. You can walk right up to them, run your hand along 3-billion-year-old Lewisian gneiss - the oldest rock in western Europe - and stand in the central circle of 13 stones around the 5-metre monolith at its heart. The site is free to visit year-round. What the builders created here is a cross-shaped layout of stone rows radiating from the central circle, positioned on a ridge overlooking Loch Roag, aligned with the extreme rising and setting points of both the sun and the moon. Every 18.6 years the full moon appears to skim so low across the southern hills that it seems to roll along the horizon - an event that was almost certainly observed and predicted here. The visitor centre is undergoing major redevelopment; until it reopens in 2026 there are no facilities on site, so arrive prepared.

A few kilometres north of Callanish, Dun Carloway Broch is the best-preserved Iron Age broch in the Western Isles - still standing 9 metres high, which gives you a real sense of what these hollow drystone towers looked like when complete. Brochs are found only in Scotland, built roughly around the 1st century AD, probably as high-status residences for tribal leaders. You can step inside, walk between the double walls, and climb the internal staircase. The classic Dun Carloway story: in the early 17th century, a raiding clan hid stolen cattle inside the tower - until the aggrieved party climbed the outer wall by inserting daggers between the stones as handholds, then dropped burning heather through the top. Free entry, small visitor centre across the road.

Two kilometres further, the Gearrannan Blackhouse Village is a cluster of nine restored thatched stone cottages perched directly above the Atlantic on the west coast of Lewis. The last residents moved out in 1974 - less than 50 years ago - after electricity arrived in 1952 and piped water in the 1960s gradually made the traditional communal way of life feel less essential. The museum cottage is restored to 1950s condition: low ceilings, box beds, a central peat fire with no chimney (the smoke filtered out through the thatch). The contrast between what life here looked like and what was happening in mainland Britain at the same time is quietly astonishing. The café and shop reopen in 2026.

activities.text: An Lanntair arts centre, on South Beach, is a glass-fronted building overlooking the harbour with a cinema, gallery, café and regular live Gaelic music and traditional ceilidh nights. Even if you're only passing through town, check what's on - this is the cultural heartbeat of the islands and an evening here is completely different from anything you'd find on the mainland. The centre is open daily except Sundays.

The loop road between Callanish and Carloway also passes Arnol, where the preserved Arnol Blackhouse (Historic Environment Scotland) shows an even earlier version of the same traditional dwelling - this one with the cattle stall at one end still intact, and a peat fire still burning inside as it would have been for centuries. If you're only going to one blackhouse, this is the more atmospheric of the two.

The Butt of Lewis, at the very northern tip of the island, is a 30-minute drive from Stornoway on the A857. The lighthouse here was built in 1862, and the cliffs below it take the full force of Atlantic storms with nothing between them and Canada. On a rough day this is one of the most dramatic spots in Scotland. On a calm day, look for gannets diving offshore.

days_plan.itin_title: Outer Hebrides Road Trip: Lewis, Harris and the Island Chain
Outer Hebrides Road Trip: Lewis, Harris and the Island Chain

map_keywords: Lews Castle Museum nan Eilean Stornoway
Callanish Standing Stones Isle of Lewis

visits.text: Stow-on-the-Wold is the Cotswolds' highest town, perched at 244 metres on the Roman Fosse Way. Its Market Square, which has hosted markets since 1107, once saw up to 20,000 sheep at a single fair—the 'tures', those narrow stone-walled lanes, were used to funnel flocks toward the square. Today, start at the Cotswold Cheese Company on Digbeth Street: over 120 artisan cheeses are stocked, with a strong focus on local producers. Try the Double Gloucester from Smart's Farm, whose tradition goes back to local cheese competitions.

Six kilometres from Stow, Daylesford Organic Farm Shop is a national reference for organic and sustainable farming. You'll find pasture-raised meat, artisan bread, house-made cheeses and an exceptional deli. The farm also has a restaurant, creamery and cookery school. If the budget allows, lunch on site is a fine way to taste what the Cotswolds terroir can deliver.

Don't miss Stow's farmers' market, held on the second Thursday of each month on Market Square from 9am to 1pm. Oils, jams, breads, meats and cheeses direct from local producers—this is where 'zero food miles' makes complete sense in the Cotswolds.

activities.text: An artisan cheese tasting at the Cotswold Cheese Company can be improvised easily with help from the cheesemongers—they'll guide you through the differences between young and aged Double Gloucester, Shropshire Blue and local goat's cheese. Then assemble a platter to take away for a picnic in the Cotswolds countryside.

The gastropub meal is a Cotswolds institution. The Porch House on Digbeth Street claims the title of Britain's oldest inn, with beams carbon-dated to over 1,000 years. Cotswolds lamb regularly features on the menu—raised on local pastures, the meat is finer and more flavourful than average.

days_plan.itin_title: Cotswolds & Oxfordshire Tour: Golden Stone Villages & University City
England's Gastronomic Trail: Markets, Pubs & Culinary Heritage
England Grand Tour: From Cornwall to the Northern Lakes

visits.text: Begin with Shakespeare's Birthplace on Henley Street: a 15th-century timber-framed house where William was born in 1564. His father John was a wool merchant and glover, and the front half of the house served as his workshop. Shakespeare inherited the property on his father's death in 1601 and rented it out as an inn. The bedroom where he was born is open to visitors, with a copy of the 1623 First Folio—the collection that saved 18 of his plays from oblivion.

Anne Hathaway's Cottage, 1.5km from the centre in Shottery, is the farmhouse where Shakespeare's wife grew up. The beams and period furniture are largely authentic. William married Anne in 1582, aged 18 (she was 26). The garden, recreated from plants mentioned in Shakespeare's plays, is especially pleasant in spring. You can walk there from the centre in 20 minutes.

Nash's House and the site of New Place occupy the location of Stratford's grandest house, purchased by Shakespeare in 1597. The house was demolished in 1759 by an owner exasperated by tourists coming to admire the mulberry tree planted by the playwright—he tore up the tree and razed the house, and the town never forgave him. Archaeological excavations have since uncovered the foundations.

Hall's Croft is the Tudor house where Shakespeare's eldest daughter lived with her physician husband John Hall. The collection of 17th-century medical equipment—and herbal remedies—gives a striking sense of Elizabethan medicine. The adjoining garden is one of the most tranquil in the town centre.

Holy Trinity Church, by the Avon, is the church where Shakespeare was baptised on 26 April 1564 and where he's buried before the chancel. The inscription on his tomb remains famous: 'Good friend for Jesus sake forbear, to dig the dust enclosed here'—a plea that his remains not be moved, as was customary for overcrowded churchyards. Among the chancel windows, some came from Antwerp and date from the 15th century.

Mary Arden's Farm in Wilmcote (5km north) is the 16th-century farm where Shakespeare's mother grew up. The estate still operates as a working Tudor farm, with heritage breed animals and craft demonstrations. It's the most rural and most living of the five Shakespeare Birthplace Trust sites.

activities.text: A boat or canoe trip on the Avon from Stratford is one of the region's most pleasant activities. The river flows between meadows and wooded banks, and you'll glimpse Holy Trinity Church from the water along the way. Rowboat and canoe hire is available on the Avon banks two minutes' walk from the centre.

The Royal Shakespeare Theatre programmes Shakespeare performances year-round. If you've planned your visit ahead, an evening at the RSC is an experience you won't find anywhere else. Tickets sell fast in summer—book several weeks in advance.

days_plan.itin_title: The Midlands and Peak District Essentials

visits.text: Journey to Brownsea Island, a National Trust nature reserve in the heart of Poole Harbour that is home to one of Britain's few remaining colonies of red squirrels. This 500-acre island of woodland, heathland and lagoons also holds historical significance as the birthplace of the worldwide Scouting movement, where Baden-Powell held his first camp in 1907. Peacocks roam freely among the paths, and the diverse habitats support an abundance of wildlife including sika deer and numerous bird species.

Explore the island's varied landscapes on self-guided walking trails that reveal different ecosystems from pine woodland to sandy beaches. The outdoor center provides information about the wildlife you might encounter, from kingfishers and ospreys to the island's famous red squirrels. The tranquil atmosphere and lack of vehicles create a peaceful escape just minutes from the bustling mainland.

Discover Swanage, a charming Victorian seaside town that evolved from a smugglers' haven into a beloved family holiday destination during the 19th century. The sandy beach, traditional pier and historic town center retain their old-world charm, while boat trips and coastal walks offer modern adventures. The town's quarrying heritage is visible in the stone buildings and monuments that were shipped from here to build some of London's finest structures.

Hike to Old Harry Rocks, the spectacular chalk sea stacks that stand as sentinels at the eastern gateway to the Jurassic Coast. The challenging climb from Swanage rewards hikers with views of these iconic white pillars rising from aquamarine waters, their brilliant surfaces contrasting with the deep blue sea. This UNESCO World Heritage coastline tells the story of 185 million years of Earth's history in its cliffs, rocks and fossils.

Visit Durlston Country Park, just one mile from Swanage, where the historic Great Globe and panoramic coastal views have drawn visitors since Victorian times. This 320-acre park encompasses limestone cliffs, wildflower meadows and wildlife-rich habitats with some of the best views along the Dorset coast. The castle-like Durlston Castle visitor center provides information about the park's geology, wildlife and the fascinating history of the Great Globe monument.

activities.text: Take a self-guided wildlife walk and try to spot the elusive red squirrels that thrive on this protected island
Walk the South West Coast Path from Old Harry Rocks back to Swanage beach for stunning coastal views

days_plan.itin_title: Dorset's Jurassic Coast Guide: Cliffs, Corfe Castle & Coastal Walks
Dorset's Jurassic Coast Guide: Cliffs, Corfe Castle & Coastal Walks

places.name: Tarbert Harris

visits.text: The drive south from Stornoway to Tarbert - about 60 km - crosses the invisible boundary between Lewis and Harris somewhere in the mountains of North Harris, where the landscape shifts entirely. Lewis is peat bog and sky; Harris is rock and mountain. The A859 climbs through bare granite scenery that feels Icelandic. Tarbert sits at the narrow isthmus where East and West Loch Tarbert almost meet, bisecting the island - it's where you catch the CalMac ferry to Skye if you need to. The town has a handful of shops, a couple of good cafés and the distillery.

The Isle of Harris Distillery, opened in October 2015, was the first legal distillery ever built on Harris. It was funded partly by a £2.8 million grant and the investment of the entire island community - the original team of ten local people all came from traditional trades: Harris Tweed weavers, crofters, fishermen. Their Harris Gin is made with 9 botanicals including sugar kelp hand-harvested from the local waters and comes in a distinctive blue bottle that won a Gold Award at the World Gin Awards in 2021. Their single malt whisky, The Hearach (Gaelic for 'a person from Harris'), is now in production and the first limited releases are available. Tours run Monday to Thursday - book ahead.

Luskentyre (Traigh Losgaintir) is the most photographed beach in the Outer Hebrides, and the photographs do not exaggerate. At low tide the bay opens into a vast expanse of white shell sand with water that sits somewhere between aquamarine and turquoise - so startlingly Caribbean in colour that first-time visitors routinely assume their eyes are playing tricks. The island of Taransay sits low on the horizon a mile offshore. The mountains of North Harris rise directly behind. The beach is reached by a 3-mile single-track road off the A859; come at low tide and on a sunny day if you can. Toilets at the car park - this is the only beach on Harris with them. No other facilities, so bring food and water. Check the tide tables before you leave Tarbert.

Seilebost beach, immediately south of Luskentyre on the other side of the estuary, is smaller but many photographers prefer it - the view back across to Luskentyre with the Harris hills behind is the classic shot. A few hundred metres further, Scarista (Sgarasta Mhor) is a quieter alternative, nearly as large as Luskentyre, with a golf course running along the dunes and Cleit Rock standing sentinel above the beach. These three beaches form one of the most extraordinary stretches of Atlantic coastline in Europe.

Leverburgh (An t-Ob) at the southern tip of Harris is a small but lively harbour from which the CalMac ferry runs across the Sound of Harris to Berneray and North Uist. The 1-hour crossing threads through a labyrinth of skerries and tiny islands - it's a remarkable piece of navigation and a proper Hebridean ferry experience. Book your crossing in advance; the Sound of Harris ferry is popular and can fill up. Leverburgh also has a good community shop and a couple of places to eat - worth stopping for lunch before or after the beach loop.

activities.text: An Cliseam (Clisham), at 799 metres, is the highest peak in the Outer Hebrides. The ascent from the A859 near Ardvourlie takes about 3 to 4 hours return and offers panoramic views over Harris, Lewis and - on a clear day - as far as St Kilda 60 km offshore. The path is rough and boggy in places; proper hill walking gear is essential.

For a shorter afternoon outing, the Golden Road - the B887 which winds along the rocky east coast of South Harris - is one of the most dramatic drives in the Outer Hebrides. The road earns its nickname not from scenery but from the cost of building it through such difficult terrain. Every turn reveals a different configuration of lochans, rocky outcrops and sea views. Stop wherever you like - there are no crowds.

The walk to Huisinis beach, at the very end of the B887 on the north-west peninsula of Harris, is one of the island's best-kept secrets. The beach is small and sheltered, but the 20-minute walk from the parking area passes over moorland with views of the St Kilda archipelago on the horizon on clear days. Wildlife at Huisinis includes grey seals on the rocks below and eagles over the ridge.

days_plan.itin_title: Outer Hebrides Road Trip: Lewis, Harris and the Island Chain
Outer Hebrides Road Trip: Lewis, Harris and the Island Chain

map_keywords: An Cliseam Clisham Harris highest point

visits.text: Tate Britain, opened in 1897 on the banks of the Thames at Millbank, houses the world's largest collection of works by J.M.W. Turner (1775-1851). Turner bequeathed to the nation nearly 300 oil paintings and some 30,000 watercolours and works on paper - the Clore Gallery, accessible from the main entrance, displays about a hundred works permanently, free of charge. If you only know Turner for his misty seascapes, you'll be surprised: he also painted the 1834 fire of Parliament from the Thames bank, and his painting documenting the scene is one of the museum's most striking.

Beyond Turner, Tate Britain covers British art from the 16th century to today - Hogarth, Constable, Bacon, Hockney. If you're there in summer, the central Duveen Hall is a monumental neoclassical nave entrusted each year to an artist for a temporary and free installation.

activities.text: Take the Tate Boat from Millbank Pier - it links Tate Britain to Tate Modern along the Thames, stopping at the London Eye. It's an original way to connect the two museums in 20 minutes, with views of both banks. The boat ticket includes a discount at the other Tate.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries
What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

visits.text: Tate Modern has occupied the former Bankside Power Station since 2000, built between 1947 and 1963 by architect Giles Gilbert Scott - the same man who designed the red London telephone box. The power station closed in 1981 after oil price increases. Swiss architects Herzog & de Meuron won the conversion competition in 1995 from 148 candidates: their masterstroke was preserving the turbine hall intact, an industrial nave 155 metres long and 35 metres high, now dedicated to gigantic installations. Admission to the permanent collections is free.

The Blavatnik Building extension, opened in 2016, increased exhibition space by 60%. Go up to level 10 for the free panoramic terrace - you have St Paul's facing you, Tower Bridge to the right, the City to the left. It's one of the best free viewpoints in London.

activities.text: Cross the Millennium Bridge on foot - this pedestrian bridge, inaugurated in 2000 and closed two days later due to excessive vibrations (reopened in 2002 after correction), directly links Tate Modern to St Paul's Cathedral on the north bank. The view from the middle of the bridge, with St Paul's on one side and Tate on the other, is one of London's finest.

Shakespeare's Globe Theatre is a 5-minute walk east of Tate Modern. This reconstruction of the 1599 Elizabethan theatre opened in 1997 and presents open-air performances from April to October. You can stand in the 'groundling pit' for cheaper access. Even off-season, the guided tour of the theatre is enlightening.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries
What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

places.name: Tenby

visits.text: Rhossili Bay is a 5 km Atlantic sand beach, closed to the north by the Llangennith dunes and to the south by the Worm's Head promontory. From the cliffs of Rhossili village, the view across the entire bay is one of the most photographed in Wales. The National Trust car park at the top of the cliff is often full at weekends in summer—arrive before 10am or after 5pm.

Worm's Head is a marshy island shaped like a dragon—worm comes from the Old Norse ormr, serpent or dragon—connected to the mainland by a rocky causeway accessible only 2h30 either side of low tide. Several people have been trapped by the tide trying to return on foot, including the poet Dylan Thomas himself, stranded there for a whole night. The National Coastwatch Institute office, at the start of the causeway, displays tide times: check them without fail before crossing. Grey seals regularly haul out on the rocks.

The Helvetia wreck is one of Gower's most famous. This Norwegian three-master was carrying 500 tonnes of timber from Canada to Swansea when a storm forced it to take refuge in Rhossili Bay on 1 November 1887. The anchors didn't hold, the wind shifted, the ship ran aground on the sand—the entire crew survived. The wooden ribs of the hull, buried in the sand for over 130 years, appear and disappear depending on storms and tides. Visible at low tide, about 30 minutes' walk from the cliff path.

Tenby is a medieval town whose walls are among the best preserved in Britain—Grade I listed, they form an almost complete triangle around the old quarter. The first stones date back to the 13th century, under the Earls of Pembroke. In 1260, the Welsh prince Llewelyn ap Gruffydd sacked the town: the new walls, higher and better reinforced, date from this reconstruction. Jasper Tudor—uncle of the future Henry VII—had them repaired and raised in 1457. Enter through the Five Arches, the D-shaped barbican gate still standing on the east side, and lose yourself in the cobbled lanes sloping down to the harbour.

St Mary's Church in Tenby is one of the largest medieval churches in Wales. Inside, look for the memorial to Robert Recorde—a Tenby native, this 16th-century mathematician invented the '=' sign. Another memorial is dedicated to Mayor Thomas White, who, according to local tradition, helped Jasper Tudor and the young Henry Tudor flee the town through underground tunnels in 1471 to reach the harbour and sail to Brittany—a journey that preceded Henry's conquest of the English throne in 1485.

The Tudor Merchant's House, at the bottom of Quay Hill, is a 15th-century house run by the National Trust. A wealthy Tenby merchant's Tudor-era dwelling is carefully recreated—period furniture, tapestries and fragments of original frescoes. Allow 30 minutes.

Castle Hill, at the tip of the promontory, offers the classic Tenby panorama: the pastel houses of the harbour to the left, North Beach to the right, and offshore the silhouette of St Catherine's Island with its Victorian fort. This island played the role of a high-security prison in Sherlock series 4. The Tenby Museum (Wales' oldest independent museum, opened in 1878) is housed in the ruins of the medieval castle at the summit.

Bosherston Lily Ponds are a network of three artificial lake arms created in the 18th century by the Cawdor family by damming an estuary—an idea born after a visitor noticed how a high tide transformed the valley into a romantic lake. Now a National Nature Reserve managed by the National Trust, the ponds are covered with white water lilies in June and July. Over 20 species of dragonfly have been recorded here, otters live here, and herons constantly perch on the jetties. The circular walk around the ponds (3 km) is flat and accessible to all.

Broad Haven South, at the end of the ponds, is an immense beach—golden sand, dunes, and in the centre a limestone monolith isolated from the sea called Church Rock, rising like a cathedral. It's a serious bodyboarding beach in swell conditions; in calm weather, the water is transparent and shallow.

Barafundle Bay is regularly ranked among Europe's most beautiful beaches. Nestled between cliffs and accessible only on foot (15 minutes from Stackpole Quay), it never suffers from the overcrowding of beaches with adjacent parking. Golden sand, turquoise water in fine weather, no facilities on the beach itself—bring your picnic. The stone arch with the National Trust sign above the beach is one of Pembrokeshire's most recognisable images.

At the end of the day, push on to St Govan's Chapel: a tiny 13th-century chapel (possibly older) wedged in a vertical fissure in the coastal cliffs at the bottom of a 52-step staircase. A hermit is said to have lived there, according to legend surrounded by rocks that closed in to protect him from his enemies. Check before leaving if the area is open—it's sometimes closed for military exercises (Castlemartin firing range).

activities.text: The walk to the end of Worm's Head (about 1.5 km from the causeway) gives access to a natural arch, a blowhole and seabird colonies. Allow a minimum of 2h30 on the island to have time to return before the tide cuts off the passage. Don't attempt the crossing if the sea is rough.

Sunset at Rhossili is renowned throughout the Gower Peninsula. From the clifftop above the beach, you watch the sun descend directly into the sea with Worm's Head in the foreground. Bring a picnic and wait—it's worth the patience.

The boat crossing to Caldey Island takes 20 minutes from Tenby harbour. This private island has been inhabited by Cistercian monks since 1929, who produce their own chocolate and perfumes made from herbs. Access is seasonal (generally April to October). Outside ferry hours, tour Tenby's three main beaches (North, South and Castle Beach) by following the clifftop path.

The Bosherston-Broad Haven South-Barafundle Bay coastal loop is the best circuit for this day: 10 km from the Stackpole Quay or Bosherston car park, alternating between paths around the ponds and clifftop coastal path. The section between Broad Haven South and Barafundle passes Stackpole Head, a limestone promontory with rock stacks in the sea. Allow 3 hours. In June-July, the lily pads on the ponds are in flower—don't miss this moment.

days_plan.itin_title: Wales Road Trip: Conwy Castle, Pembrokeshire Coast & Snowdonia
South Wales Circuit: Mountains and Coast
South Wales Circuit: Mountains and Coast
South Wales Circuit: Mountains and Coast
