visits.text: Chavenage House, Elizabethan manor.
Castle Combe, perfect village often filmed.
Route to Bath via rolling countryside.

activities.text: Free exploration of Bath city center in the evening.
Morning spa session at Thermae Bath Spa.

days_plan.itin_title: Cotswolds & Oxfordshire Tour: Golden Stone Villages & University City

visits.text: The Shard is designed by Italian architect Renzo Piano, who conceived it as a 95-storey 'vertical city'. The building stands 309.6 metres tall and has dominated London's skyline since its inauguration on 5 July 2012. The viewing platform 'The View from The Shard' occupies levels 68 to 72 - at 244 metres, you have a 360° view extending up to 64 kilometres on a clear day. During 2011 construction, a fox moved into level 72 by entering through the stairs. It survived several weeks feeding on workers' leftovers - it's now called Romeo and is the building's official mascot. Book online, and prefer late afternoon slots for the light on Tower Bridge.

activities.text: A 5-minute walk from the Shard, Borough Market is one of London's oldest food markets - present on this site since at least the 13th century. You'll find British and European producers, cheeses, charcuterie, fresh oysters and quality street food. Open Wednesday to Saturday. Avoid Friday lunchtime - it's peak rush.

Southwark Cathedral, steps from the market, is London's oldest Gothic church - its nave dates from the 13th century. Shakespeare baptized his brother Edmund there in 1607. Admission is free and the serenity of the place contrasts radically with the market bustle just outside.

days_plan.itin_title: What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

places.name: Thornton Le Dale

visits.text: Knaresborough sets the tone from arrival: limestone houses cling to a cliff above the River Nidd, and the Victorian viaduct spans the horizon like a film backdrop. Walk down to the water's edge for the classic view of boats and colorful facades—this is where the town truly makes sense, not from above.

Knaresborough Castle has overlooked the Nidd since the 14th century. Built by the Normans, dismantled after the English Civil War on Cromwell's orders in 1648, only the square keep remains—but the view from the gardens perched above the cliff is remarkable. Allow about 45 minutes for the integrated museum tracing the town's history.

Don't miss Mother Shipton's Cave, officially England's oldest paying attraction, open to the public since 1630. The site comprises two wonders side by side along the Nidd: the cave where the prophetess Ursula Southeil was supposedly born in 1488 (credited with predicting the Spanish Armada and the Great Fire of London), and the Petrifying Well, whose mineral-rich water gradually encrusts objects suspended in it—a teddy bear takes three to five months to 'petrify.' Allow at least an hour and buy tickets online, especially in high season.

Heading north, stop at the White Horse of Kilburn for one of Yorkshire's most sweeping views. This equine figure carved into the hillside measures 96 meters long by 70 meters high, cut through to the chalk since 1857—visible from over 30 kilometers away in clear weather. The Sutton Bank viewpoint just nearby offers a panorama across the entire Vale of York that justifies the detour alone.

Thornton-le-Dale crowns this day as it should. The village has been regularly voted Yorkshire's most beautiful—since at least 1907, when a newspaper poll bestowed that title. Walk around the village green with its 13th-century medieval cross and stocks (last documented use: 1874), then follow the stream to Beck Isle Cottage, a Grade II listed 17th-century timber-framed thatched cottage that appears on thousands of calendars and chocolate boxes. The house is private—admire it from outside, from the small stone bridge.

Before leaving the village, walk to All Saints Church on the eastern edge. The building dates to the 14th century but traces of religious occupation on the site go back to the 7th century. Inside, look for musket ball marks on the nave pillars—they date from the English Civil War, when a group of Parliamentarian soldiers sought refuge here against Royalists.

activities.text: Evening walk along Thornton Beck, the stream running through the village. The 2-mile route to the hamlet of Ellerburn and its St Hilda's church (dated to at least 1050) is peaceful and easy—allow about an hour. Best to set off just before light fades.

The village has several independent cafés and pubs around the green, ideal for a late-day stop. If you pass through in August, the Thornton-le-Dale Scarecrow Festival transforms the village into a life-size scarecrow gallery—a strangely endearing event.

days_plan.itin_title: Yorkshire's Most Beautiful Places

map_keywords: White Horse of Kilburn North Yorkshire

visits.text: Stand among the legendary ruins of Tintagel Castle, perched dramatically on a rugged headland with cliffs plunging to the Atlantic on three sides. While not much remains of the medieval castle itself, the association with King Arthur and the dramatic new footbridge spanning a deep chasm between two impossibly steep cliffs make this one of Cornwall's most unforgettable attractions. The legends claim this as Arthur's birthplace, and the atmosphere of myth and mystery pervades every stone.

Cross the spectacular footbridge that links the mainland to the island portion of Tintagel Castle, a modern engineering marvel that recreates an ancient connection severed by centuries of erosion. The vertiginous walkway offers heart-stopping views down to the waves crashing on rocks far below. Archaeological excavations have revealed that this site was an important settlement during the Dark Ages, lending some historical basis to the Arthurian legends.

Explore Merlin's Cave beneath the castle headland, accessible at low tide via a dramatic sea cave that cuts completely through the promontory. Legend holds that the wizard Merlin lived in this cave and that it was here he first met the infant Arthur. The combination of dramatic natural features and powerful mythology makes Tintagel one of the most evocative historic sites in Britain, drawing visitors who seek both history and legend.

activities.text: Book castle tickets in advance, especially during holidays and weekends, as this is one of Cornwall's most popular attractions

days_plan.itin_title: Cornwall Coastal Road Trip: Castles, Secret Coves & Celtic Heritage Guide
Southwest England: Dramatic Cliffs and Coastal Village Charm

places.name: Tobermory

visits.text: Tobermory, capital of the Isle of Mull, takes its name from the Gaelic Tobar Mhoire—'Mary's well'—referring to a sacred spring dedicated to the Virgin. The town was founded in 1788 by the British Fisheries Society as a planned herring port. The row of houses painted pink, yellow, blue, and red lining the quayside dates from the 19th century: the first building to be painted was the Mishnish Hotel in 1961, the others followed. At the harbor's edge, Tobermory Distillery has operated since 1798—don't miss the guided tour of one of Scotland's oldest commercial distilleries. For a cultural aside: the BBC series Balamory (254 episodes, 2002-2005) was filmed here. Beneath the harbor waters supposedly lies the wreck of a Spanish Armada galleon sunk in 1588—the treasure has never been found.

A few minutes' walk from the harbor, Aros Park is a natural space managed by the Forestry Commission, crossed by trails that follow streams and lead to small waterfalls within dense forest cover. Access is free. Also don't miss An Tobar Arts Centre, housed in a former school, which hosts exhibitions, live traditional music, and a café with harbor views.

Don't miss Duart Castle, a medieval fortress on a promontory facing the Sound of Mull near Craignure: built in the 13th century, passed to the MacLeans in the 14th century, abandoned as a ruin for two centuries, it was bought back and restored from 1911 onward by the 26th chief of Clan MacLean. It's one of the rare castles occupied by the same clan for 700 years. Views over the Firth of Lorn and surrounding islands. Admission charged. Book your ticket in advance during high season.

At Mull's northern tip, Calgary Bay is the island's largest white sand beach, framed by cliffs and forests. Its name inspired the Canadian city: Scottish settler families evicted during the Highland Clearances took the name with them when settling in Alberta. The bay is virtually deserted off-season: arrive in the morning to have the sand to yourself.

activities.text: Offshore from Mull, the waters of the Sound of Mull rank among the best marine wildlife viewing areas in the UK. Boat trips depart from Tobermory to observe dolphins, gray seals, minke whales, and white-tailed sea eagles that nest on the island following their reintroduction. Local operators generally depart in the morning for best conditions. Book ahead in season.

Ben More (966 m) is the only Munro in the Inner Hebrides outside Skye. The ascent from the shore of Loch na Keal takes between 5 and 7 hours round trip. If you're not equipped for the summit, hiking in the surrounding valleys is still spectacular. On clear days, you can glimpse as far as Ireland from the top.

The island of Ulva—accessible by a short ferry crossing from Ulva Ferry pier on Mull's west coast—offers an off-the-beaten-track excursion. The island has fewer than 20 residents. You'll see ruins of dwellings from the 700 people who populated it before the Clearances. The ferry operates on demand—wave the sign on the shore and someone will come fetch you.

days_plan.itin_title: Scotland Road Trip: Edinburgh, Highlands & Isle of Skye
Scotland Road Trip: Edinburgh, Highlands & Isle of Skye

map_keywords: Calgary Bay beach Isle of Mull

places.name: Tongue

visits.text: Cape Wrath is the most north-westerly point of the British mainland - and getting there requires a small ferry across the Kyle of Durness, followed by an 11-mile minibus ride over military training ground. The route is managed by a single operator and runs April to October, weather permitting. At the cape, the Stevenson lighthouse of 1828 stands above 280-metre cliffs of Torridonian sandstone, the same ancient rock as the Torridon mountains. On a clear day the Orkney islands are visible 45 miles north-east. The cliffs below the lighthouse hold one of the largest mainland gannet colonies in Britain. The minibus driver's commentary is part of the experience - book in advance.

Smoo Cave, 2 km east of Durness on the A838, is the largest sea cave entrance in Britain - 40 metres wide and 15 metres high. What makes it unusual within the UK is that the first chamber was formed by sea action while the inner chambers are freshwater passages, carved by the Allt Smoo stream dissolving the dolomite limestone. They are entirely separate geological processes that happen to have joined up. A 20-metre waterfall drops through a hole in the inner chamber's ceiling. You can walk to the first chamber and waterfall platform for free at any time; guided boat tours of the inner chamber run April to September. The name is Old Norse - 'smjúga' or 'smuga', meaning a narrow hole or hiding place.

Balnakeil beach, 2 km west of Durness, is a wide arc of white sand on a sheltered bay that is surprisingly tropical-looking when the sun is on the water. A ruined 17th-century church stands at the back of the beach; the grave of Donald McMurchow, a murderer who paid for the church's construction in exchange for a burial inside the walls rather than in unconsecrated ground, is still visible. The Balnakeil craft village - a collection of former Cold War early warning station buildings converted into artists' studios and workshops - is worth a wander.

activities.text: The Kyle of Tongue causeway, carrying the A838 across the tidal Kyle, is one of the most photographed spots on the north coast. Pull over at the parking area at the southern end and look north: the castle ruin of Castle Varrich on its hilltop, the causeway striping the blue water, the cone of Ben Loyal (764 m) - sometimes called the Queen of Scottish Mountains - in the background. Spend 20 minutes here.

days_plan.itin_title: North Coast 500 with Orkney: Scotland's Ultimate Coastal Circuit

map_keywords: Kyle of Tongue causeway Ben Loyal

visits.text: The NC500 officially starts and ends at Inverness Castle, but the route makes more sense driven clockwise - west first, east last, saving the dramatic coast of Caithness and the east Highland passage for the final days. Head south-west on the A82 and A87 toward Kyle of Lochalsh. You'll pass through Eilean Donan Castle country - the most photographed castle in Scotland, perched on a tiny island where three lochs converge, built in the 13th century to guard against Viking raids, blown up in 1719 when Spanish troops supporting the Jacobites stored 343 barrels of gunpowder inside, then rebuilt stone by stone from 1919 to 1932. Stop for the view from the road bridge; the castle entrance itself is worth 45 minutes if you have time.

From Kyle of Lochalsh, take the A896 north into Glen Torridon. This valley is one of the most geologically extraordinary places in Scotland: the mountains on either side are Torridonian sandstone, around 750 million years old, sitting directly on Lewisian gneiss that is 3 billion years old. Almost nowhere else in the world does rock of such different ages sit in direct contact. Beinn Eighe (1,010 m) and Liathach (1,055 m) loom over the road. The National Trust for Scotland manages a deer museum at Torridon village - worth a 20-minute stop to understand the landscape before walking in it.

activities.text: The Torridon hills offer some of the finest - and most serious - ridge walking in Scotland. The approach to Liathach from the road up the glen is for experienced hillwalkers only; the summit ridge involves Grade 2 scrambling in places and the exposure is significant. For a more accessible but still dramatic walk, the path up the Coire Mhic Fhearchair on Beinn Eighe reaches a dramatic Triple Buttress cirque at 750 m - one of the grandest mountain corries in Scotland. Expect 4 to 5 hours return and proper boots.

days_plan.itin_title: North Coast 500 with Orkney: Scotland's Ultimate Coastal Circuit

visits.text: Tower Bridge was inaugurated on 30 June 1894 by the future Edward VII. Built over 8 years by 432 workers, it cost £1.18 million. Its Gothic stone appearance is an illusion: the structure is entirely steel, clad in Cornish granite and Portland stone to harmonize with the nearby Tower of London. Each bascule weighs 1,200 tonnes, with a 410-tonne counterweight allowing them to lift in 5 minutes. In 1894, the bascules lifted 6,194 times - 17 times per day. Today: about 800 times a year. Check lift times on the bridge website before your visit.

The glass walkways at 43 metres height offer a plunging view over the Thames - the glass floor installed in 2014 lets you look directly beneath your feet at the cars and river. In the Engine Rooms, the original steam engines are still in place: until 1976, 30 people worked here continuously, with over 20 tonnes of coal consumed per week.

activities.text: For the best photographs of Tower Bridge, position yourself on the south bank, east side of the bridge - you have the Tower of London in the background and morning light on the north facade. In the evening, from London Bridge (800 metres to the west), the view of illuminated Tower Bridge with the City is unbeatable.

days_plan.itin_title: What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

visits.text: The Tower of London, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1988, is one of Europe's most remarkable Norman fortresses. The White Tower—its central keep standing 27 metres tall with walls 4.5 metres thick—was built from 1078 on the orders of William the Conqueror using Caen stone imported from Normandy. It has successively served as a royal palace, armoury, prison for high-ranking prisoners and royal menagerie. Anne Boleyn was executed here, King Edward IV's two sons mysteriously disappeared here, and Guy Fawkes was tortured here after the Gunpowder Plot of 1605. Today, the Tower houses the Crown Jewels in the Jewel House: over 23,000 gemstones including the 530-carat Cullinan I diamond (the world's largest cut diamond) set in the royal sceptre, and the 105-carat Koh-i-Noor diamond in the Queen Mother's crown. The Crown Jewels have been displayed to the public since 1661—one of Britain's most visited attractions. Book tickets online in advance.

activities.text: Don't miss the guided tour by the Yeoman Warders (the Beefeaters): these guards in Tudor uniforms bring history to life with quintessentially British verve. Tours depart every 30 minutes from the main entrance—they're included in admission.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries
What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

visits.text: Nelson's Column measures 51.5 metres - exactly the distance from HMS Victory's mainmast to the main deck. This is no coincidence. The statue of Nelson at the top (5.5m in Scottish sandstone) looks towards the Admiralty, and towards Cape Trafalgar in Spain where he died in 1805. The bronze lions at the column's base weren't added until 1867 - Landseer, an animal painter (not sculptor), worked from a real lion's corpse, but decomposition had already begun: as a result, the paws resemble a cat's. Also look for the northwest fourth plinth: since 1999 it's hosted rotating contemporary artworks, often surprising.

The National Gallery has bordered the square's north side since 1838. Admission is free - which is an anomaly for a museum of this calibre. You'll find around 2,300 works: Van Eyck, Botticelli, Leonardo da Vinci, Rembrandt, Turner, Monet, Van Gogh. If you only have an hour, focus on the Sainsbury Wing rooms for Flemish and Italian primitives. Allow at least 2 hours if you want to cover the essentials.

Facing the National Gallery, spot the statue of George Washington - standing on American soil imported specially, as Washington had sworn never to set foot on British ground. It's a gift from the State of Virginia presented in 1921.

activities.text: In late afternoon, the square comes alive with street performers and musicians - the atmosphere is very different from the touristy morning. Sit on the fountain steps and watch: Trafalgar Square is one of the few places in London where all layers of the city genuinely intersect.

days_plan.itin_title: What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

visits.text: Explore Truro, Cornwall's only city, where the magnificent Victorian cathedral with its unusual three spires towers above the historic streets. While urban amenities and cultural offerings exceed those found elsewhere in the region, Truro maintains a distinctly laid-back Cornish character with its independent shops, cafes and the vibrant Lemon Street Market. The city serves as an excellent base for exploring the surrounding area while offering more significant cultural attractions.

Visit the Royal Cornwall Museum to discover the fascinating history of this unique region, from prehistoric times through the tin mining era to the present day. The museum's collections illuminate the distinctive Cornish culture and the Celtic language that sets this peninsula apart from the rest of England. Exhibitions explore the mining heritage that brought prosperity and shaped the landscape, now recognized with UNESCO World Heritage status.

Admire the Cathedral of the Blessed Virgin Mary, a Victorian Gothic masterpiece completed in 1910 that ranks among the finest 19th-century churches in England. The three soaring spires create an unmistakable silhouette above the city, while the interior features beautiful stained glass and impressive stone vaulting. The cathedral's relatively recent construction makes it unusual among England's great churches, most of which date from medieval times.

activities.text: Browse the Hall for Cornwall's performance schedule for evening entertainment options

days_plan.itin_title: Cornwall Coastal Road Trip: Castles, Secret Coves & Celtic Heritage Guide

visits.text: On the way from Brighton, detour to Hever Castle: Anne Boleyn's childhood castle, Henry VIII's second wife, lies about twenty minutes north of Tunbridge Wells. Built in the late 13th century and surrounded by a double moat, it houses original Tudor portraits and the two prayer books annotated in Anne's hand. The gardens created in the early 20th century by American millionaire William Waldorf Astor span 60 hectares and offer one of Kent's most beautiful settings.

Tunbridge Wells developed around a chalybeate spring discovered in 1606, which attracted all of English aristocracy for two centuries. The historic heart is the Pantiles—a 17th-century tree-lined colonnade bordered by shops. Its name comes from the pan tiles that originally paved the ground. Princess Anne donated £100 in 1698 to repair the paving after her son slipped. Today, the Pantiles house restaurants, markets, and open-air concerts. Look for the chalybeate spring still in operation—you can drink the iron-rich water according to a 400-year tradition.

activities.text: About twenty minutes southeast of Tunbridge Wells, Scotney Castle offers one of Kent's most romantic tableaux: the ruins of a 14th-century medieval castle in the middle of a lake, surrounded by English-style gardens planted with rhododendrons. In May-June, the blooming is spectacular. Managed by the National Trust.

Kent is England's garden—literally. Several vineyards in the region offer tastings: the Medway Valley and hills around Tunbridge Wells produce sparkling wines comparable to champagne. Plan to contact an estate in advance to arrange a visit and tasting.

days_plan.itin_title: Southeast England & Kent Circuit: White Cliffs and Secret Gardens

visits.text: Journey aboard the Talyllyn Railway, the world's first preserved railway, known affectionately as 'The Railway with a Heart of Gold.' This historic steam train, operating since 1865, carries passengers on a scenic 55-minute journey from the coastal town of Tywyn seven miles into the heart of Snowdonia National Park. The gentle pace allows time to absorb the stunning Welsh landscapes, from pastoral valleys to dramatic mountain scenery.

Travel to Nant Gwernol, the railway's terminus deep in a wooded ravine surrounded by ancient oak forests and cascading waterfalls. The narrow-gauge locomotives puff and whistle through countryside that has changed little since Victorian times, creating an authentically nostalgic experience. Walking trails from the station lead to spectacular viewpoints and the atmospheric ruins of old slate quarries.

Explore Tywyn, a traditional Welsh seaside town that serves as the gateway to the Talyllyn Railway and the southern reaches of Snowdonia. The town's long sandy beach stretches for miles, backed by dunes and offering views across Cardigan Bay to the Llŷn Peninsula. St Cadfan's Church houses the Cadfan Stone, bearing the earliest known written Welsh, connecting visitors to 1,400 years of Welsh linguistic heritage.

activities.text: Hike from Nant Gwernol station through the forest to discover hidden waterfalls and old quarry workings

days_plan.itin_title: Wales Road Trip: Conwy Castle, Pembrokeshire Coast & Snowdonia

visits.text: On the A832 toward Ullapool, stop at Gruinard Bay, which offers a large reddish-pink sand beach with turquoise waters. Opposite lies Gruinard Island—'anthrax island.' In 1942, the British government tested biological bombs with the Vollum 14578 strain of anthrax on sheep there as part of Operation Vegetarian. The island remained under quarantine for 48 years. In 1986, 280 tons of diluted formaldehyde were poured over every acre of the island. It was declared safe in 1990 and bought back for £500—the price agreed in 1942—by the heirs of the original owner.

Explore Ullapool, founded in 1788 as a herring port according to Thomas Telford's plans—the largest settlement for dozens of kilometers around, with 1,300 inhabitants. Its name likely comes from the Old Norse Ulla-Bolstadr ('Ulla's domain'). The waterfront, its white houses, and the late-day light over Loch Broom merit a long stroll.

Ullapool Museum, housed in a former Telford church from 1829, traces the village's history from Viking origins through herring fishing and the clearances. The Ceilidh Place is one of the northwest's liveliest cultural centers: café, bookshop, live music venue, and art gallery. Check the program before arriving.

The walk along the waterfront to Rhue lighthouse, 3 km northwest of Ullapool, offers views over the Summer Isles and Stac Pollaidh (613 m)—one of the northwest's most photographed mountains. The sunset from Rhue over the Summer Isles is among the Highlands' finest. Bring binoculars: otters and sea eagles are often visible from the coast.

activities.text: 20 km south of Ullapool on the A835, Corrieshalloch Gorge National Nature Reserve is the smallest of Scotland's 43 national nature reserves. The gorge is 1.5 km long, 60 meters deep, and only 10 meters wide. Its Gaelic name, Coire Shalach, means 'ugly corrie.' It formed 10,000 to 13,000 years ago during the melting of Quaternary glaciers. The Falls of Measach (46 meters) are crossed by a Victorian suspension bridge designed by Sir John Fowler—one of the engineers of the Forth Bridge. Cross the bridge for the best views of the falls, then access the viewpoint overlooking the chasm from the opposite bank. Most spectacular after rain.

Boat trips from Ullapool harbor allow you to reach the Summer Isles. Tanera Mòr, the largest, was the seat of an 18th-century herring fishery and now hosts a private post office issuing its own stamps. You can observe seals, dolphins, puffins, and gannets. Trips generally last 2 to 3 hours. Book your trip directly on the pontoon—places fill quickly in July-August.

days_plan.itin_title: Scottish Highlands Road Trip: Castles, Lochs and Wild Landscapes
Scotland Road Trip: Edinburgh, Highlands & Isle of Skye
Scotland Road Trip: Edinburgh, Highlands & Isle of Skye
North Coast 500 with Orkney: Scotland's Ultimate Coastal Circuit

visits.text: The Victoria & Albert Museum, founded in 1852 from the profits of the 1851 Great Exhibition, is the world's largest museum of decorative arts and design. Its 145 galleries across 12.5 acres house 2.3 million objects covering 5,000 years of human creativity. Its first director Henry Cole declared he wanted to make the museum 'a schoolroom for everyone'—thus inventing the concept of the museum café to encourage visits. Queen Victoria laid the foundation stone of the current building in 1899 during her last public appearance. Don't miss the Cast Courts on the ground floor: two immense halls filled with life-size plaster casts of the world's greatest artworks, including Michelangelo's David—Queen Victoria, shocked by David's anatomy, demanded that a modesty fig leaf be placed on the statue during official visits. Also seek out the Great Bed of Ware, a 16th-century bed three metres wide mentioned by Shakespeare, and the 1937 evening coat designed by Elsa Schiaparelli with a collar in the form of a bouquet created by Jean Cocteau. Allow at least 2.5 hours.

activities.text: On Friday evenings, the V&A stays open until 10pm with special events—a pleasant way to visit the galleries without daytime crowds.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries

visits.text: The Wallace Collection is one of London's best-kept secrets: a collection of 5,500 artworks housed in Hertford House, a Georgian townhouse on Manchester Square, five minutes from Oxford Street. Admission is free. The collection, assembled by four generations of Marquesses of Hertford and by Richard Wallace in the 19th century, was bequeathed to the nation by Lady Wallace in 1897 with one condition: nothing can be added or removed. The centrepiece is Fragonard's The Swing (c.1768), considered the absolute masterpiece of Rococo. A courtier had commissioned the painting from a religious painter, who refused the overly licentious subject and suggested Fragonard—the scene shows a woman on a swing kicking off her shoe, allowing her lover to admire beneath her skirts while her husband remains in shadow. This painting relaunched Fragonard's career. Right next to it, Frans Hals' The Laughing Cavalier (1624): this portrait of an unknown 26-year-old man painted with brushstrokes of stunning modernity was purchased in 1865 by the 4th Marquess of Hertford outbidding a Rothschild. The gallery also includes works by Rembrandt, Velázquez, Rubens, Poussin and a remarkable armoury.

activities.text: Free guided tours of the Great Gallery are offered at 3pm Friday through Sunday. It's the most efficient way to understand the major works in 30 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries

visits.text: Warwick Castle is one of England's best-preserved medieval castles. Æthelflæd, daughter of King Alfred the Great, founded a fort here in 914 to repel the Danes. William the Conqueror converted it to a stone castle in 1068. Caesar's Tower rises to 45 metres, Guy's Tower to 39 metres. The 'Kingmaker' Richard Neville, the Earl of Warwick who played English kings according to his interests in the 15th century, held court here. Canaletto painted the castle five times—making it the English building most depicted by the Venetian painter. Allow a full half-day.

Warwick's historic centre is compact and pleasant to explore on foot from the castle. The Lord Leycester Hospital, founded in 1571 as a retirement home for wounded soldiers, is a splendid ensemble of timber-framed buildings that frames a medieval gate. The great church of St Mary contains the Beauchamp Chapel—one of England's richest perpendicular Gothic chapels.

Ten kilometres from Warwick, Kenilworth Castle clearly merits the detour—it's England's largest castle ruin and one of the most romantic. Its great Norman keep dates from the 12th century; the artificial lake that once surrounded it, now drained, was medieval England's largest artificial lake. Picture the scene: Elizabeth I stayed here for 19 days in 1575, welcomed by her favourite Robert Dudley in festivities that cost the equivalent of several million modern pounds—fireworks, banquets, jousts. Walter Scott was so taken with it that he drew his novel 'Kenilworth' (1821) from the story. Allow 1.5 hours to tour it.

Royal Leamington Spa, 5km from Warwick, is the Midlands' most elegant spa town. The 19th-century Pump Rooms are now an art gallery and cultural centre. Jephson Gardens, designed by Victorian gardener Edward Milner, are among the Midlands' best-maintained urban gardens. Regent Street and The Parade are lined with Regency facades.

activities.text: The River Avon runs alongside Warwick Castle for several kilometres—local operators offer canoe or rowboat trips from the banks of the castle park. It's the ideal angle to see Caesar's and Guy's towers reflected in the water, from the same perspective as Canaletto's 18th-century paintings. Allow 1.5 hours for a leisurely outing.

The River Leam path between Leamington Spa and Warwick (5km) follows the wooded banks between the two towns—a fine walk or cycle linking the two historic centres. Start from Jephson Gardens and follow the river up to the castle walls.

days_plan.itin_title: The Midlands and Peak District Essentials

places.name: Wells Next The

visits.text: Holkham Hall is one of England's great Palladian country houses, and it deserves a morning. Thomas Coke, 1st Earl of Leicester, began it in 1734—his inspiration went back to a six-year Grand Tour of Italy (1712–1718) during which he met Lord Burlington and architect William Kent, but he waited sixteen years before launching the project. Construction took 25 years and required demolishing the old village of Holkham to clear the space. Enter the Marble Hall: its ionic columns in alabaster and its 12-metre coffered ceiling are among the most imposing neoclassical interiors in the country. After the visit, walk to Holkham beach behind the estate—three kilometres of pristine sand bordered by pines, almost always deserted outside summer weekends. This is where the final scene of Shakespeare in Love (1998) was filmed.

Burnham Thorpe, six kilometres from Holkham, is a village of 144 inhabitants that has the immense merit of being the birthplace of Horatio Nelson, born in 1758 at the parsonage—the house was demolished in 1803, but a plaque marks the spot. Nelson's father, the Reverend Edmund Nelson, was rector of All Saints church for 48 years. Inside the church, the lectern, altar and rood screen were carved from timber from the frigate HMS Victory. Nelson lived here from 1786 to 1793 while waiting to be assigned to a new ship, and organised a final farewell dinner for the villagers before embarking on HMS Agamemnon.

Wells-next-the-Sea is the liveliest port on Norfolk's north coast, with its fishing boats and colourful bathing huts lined up on the embankment. Stroll along the quay in the morning when the boats come in, then walk the ten minutes to the beach, which opens onto wide stretches of sand bordered by pines. The town has neat Georgian architecture and several character pubs along the Quay.

Cromer, 45 kilometres to the east, is Norfolk's emblematic Victorian seaside resort, with its 1901 pier and its theatre at the end of the jetty still operating—one of the few 'end-of-the-pier shows' that survive in England. St Peter and St Paul church has the tallest church tower in Norfolk (49 metres). Cromer is also famous for its crabs, renowned across the country: stop at the market or in a fisherman's shop to buy some.

activities.text: Seal-watching at Blakeney Point: Blakeney Point is home to Britain's largest colony of common and grey seals. Pleasure boats from Morston Quay (five kilometres from Wells) allow you to approach within a few metres. The colony numbers up to 3,000 individuals depending on the season.

days_plan.itin_title: East Anglia Road Trip: Norfolk, Suffolk and Cambridge
From Cambridge to London: Eastern England in 10 Days

map_keywords: Burnham Thorpe All Saints Church Nelson birthplace

visits.text: Marvel at Durdle Door, the iconic natural limestone arch that has become the symbol of the Jurassic Coast and one of England's most photographed landmarks. This spectacular geological formation, created over millions of years as the sea eroded the Portland limestone, frames turquoise waters that rival any Mediterranean cove. The dramatic cliffside setting and crystal-clear waters draw visitors year-round, though the beach is most magical outside peak summer season.

Explore Lulworth Cove, a almost perfectly circular bay that is considered one of the finest examples of coastal erosion in the world and a premier destination for geology enthusiasts. The unique formation occurred as the sea breached the harder Portland limestone and then eroded the softer rocks behind, creating this remarkable natural amphitheater. The Fossil Forest on the cliff path reveals petrified tree stumps from ancient forests that grew here 150 million years ago.

Walk the spectacular coastal path connecting Lulworth Cove to Durdle Door, one of the most scenic short hikes in all of England. The route crosses dramatic chalk cliffs with ever-changing views of the turquoise waters and rocky coastline below. This stretch of the Jurassic Coast reveals layers of geological history spanning 185 million years, visible in the multi-colored cliff faces and embedded fossils.

activities.text: Try coasteering, rock-pooling or swimming in the crystal-clear waters of this UNESCO World Heritage coastline

days_plan.itin_title: Dorset's Jurassic Coast Guide: Cliffs, Corfe Castle & Coastal Walks

visits.text: Westminster Abbey was built from 1245 on the orders of Henry III, who wanted to rival the great Gothic cathedrals of France. Since William the Conqueror on 25 December 1066, 40 monarchs have been crowned here - the only exceptions are Edward V, likely murdered in the Tower of London before his ceremony, and Edward VIII, who abdicated before the scheduled date. More than 3,300 people are buried here, including 30 sovereigns. Plan at least 90 minutes to see it properly, and take the audio guide - without it, you'll miss a lot.

Don't miss Poets' Corner in the south transept: Chaucer, Shakespeare (memorial), Dickens, Tennyson, Kipling are commemorated here. Dickens ended up there against his wishes - he'd requested a quiet burial in Kent. The Dean of Westminster orchestrated the move to add prestige to the place. Lord Byron was refused for his 'scandalous life' - his memorial stone wasn't laid until 1969, 145 years after his death.

The Chapter House, accessible from the cloisters, houses Britain's oldest door - dated to the 1050s through dendrochronology. The cloisters themselves are the only free access to the Abbey: if budget is tight, at least enter through there to soak up the atmosphere.

activities.text: Attend Evensong if your timing allows - the evening choral service is free and open to all. It's an experience with nothing touristy about it: you're in a working abbey, with a professional choir, in one of England's most beautiful Gothic naves. Check timings on the Abbey website before you go.

days_plan.itin_title: What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

visits.text: Fall in love with Weymouth, a traditional seaside town whose Georgian esplanade and golden sands have made it one of Britain's most popular holiday destinations for over two centuries. The colorful houses lining the harbor, their doors decorated with nautical knockers, create a quintessentially English coastal scene. The shallow, gently shelving beach and calm waters make this an ideal destination for families and swimmers of all abilities.

Stroll along Weymouth's historic harbor, where boats and yachts bob at their moorings and fresh seafood restaurants serve the day's catch. The old town's narrow streets reveal Georgian architecture and maritime heritage at every turn, from the harbor-side fish market to the Tudor-era waterfront buildings. The working harbor atmosphere, with fishermen mending nets and boats preparing to sail, connects visitors to Weymouth's enduring maritime traditions.

Walk along Chesil Beach, the remarkable 18-mile shingle bank connecting Portland to the Dorset mainland that is one of Britain's most distinctive coastal features. This vast barrier beach, accumulated over thousands of years, grades from small pebbles in the west to large cobbles in the east, a natural phenomenon that allowed local fishermen to determine their position in fog simply by the size of the stones. The Fleet lagoon behind the beach supports rare wildlife and offers peaceful walking away from the crowds.

activities.text: Sample fresh Dorset seafood, including the local specialty mackerel, at one of the harbor-side establishments

days_plan.itin_title: Dorset's Jurassic Coast Guide: Cliffs, Corfe Castle & Coastal Walks

visits.text: Robin Hood's Bay, 8 kilometres south of Whitby, is one of England's most beautiful coastal villages—and the most notorious. In the 18th century, it was the Yorkshire coast's most active smuggling hub: gin, brandy, tobacco and silk arrived from the Netherlands and France, and legend has it that a bale of silk could cross the village from harbour to clifftop without ever leaving the houses, thanks to underground tunnels linking the cottages. Customs officers attempting searches sometimes had buckets of boiling water thrown at them from first-floor windows. Locals call the village 'Bay Town'. Leave your car at the top—only residents can drive down to the harbour—and plunge into the lanes on foot.

Robin Hood's Bay Museum, in an old house near the harbour, displays fossils found on local beaches, fishing gear and documents on smuggling and shipwrecks. Small and free, it gives context to a walk through the village. Half an hour suffices.

Whitby Abbey, perched on the east cliff 70 metres above the harbour, is the most photogenic Gothic ruin on England's north coast. The site has been inhabited for over 3,000 years: in 657, Hilda—an abbess of royal blood, great-great-niece of Northumbria's first Christian king—founded the first monastery here, which hosted the famous Synod of Whitby in 664—the meeting that decided the English Church would follow the Roman Easter calendar rather than the Celtic one. The current Benedictine abbey dates from the 12th century, its limestone Gothic arches from the 13th. In 1890, Bram Stoker holidayed in Whitby and found in the town library the name of a 15th-century Wallachian prince nicknamed 'Dracula'. The rest is literary history: published in 1897, Dracula remains the defining Gothic novel and has never been out of print. Managed by English Heritage. Allow 1h30.

St Mary's Church, Whitby, right next to the abbey, is one of England's most singular churches: founded in the 12th century, it was expanded over the centuries by local seafaring families who claimed the interior with their own private box pews, creating an utterly unique over-stuffed wooden interior. It was in the surrounding graveyard that Stoker noted names on the tombstones—including 'Swales', the name of Dracula's first victim in the novel.

The 199 steps leading from the old town at the foot of the cliff up to the abbey have been carved into the sandstone since the 17th century. In Stoker's novel, it's by bounding up these steps in the form of a great black dog that Dracula reaches the graveyard after his ship wrecks in the harbour. The climb takes 10 minutes and comes with progressively beautiful views over Whitby's red rooftops and the Esk estuary.

The Captain Cook Memorial Museum, in Grape Lane, occupies the house of shipowner John Walker where the young James Cook lived and studied navigation from 1746 to 1749, before joining the Royal Navy. It was here, late at night by candlelight, that he learned algebra, trigonometry and astronomy. The house has remained almost as it was in the 18th century—the rooms, the attic where Cook slept, original engravings from his voyages. Allow an hour.

The Dracula Experience, on Marine Parade, takes you through a dramatised tour of Stoker's novel via sound-scapes and special effects in the cellars of an old building. The address itself supposedly has history: one of the earliest known deeds to the property reportedly bears Isaac Newton's signature—a local anecdote that Whitby guides repeat, though it can't quite be verified. Allow 45 minutes for the tour.

Hayburn Wyke waterfall, 10 kilometres south of Whitby, cascades directly onto a pebble beach surrounded by ancient woodland—one of the very few coastal waterfalls in England. The access path starts from the Hayburn Wyke Inn car park and descends through the woods in 25 minutes. The beach pebbles sometimes yield ammonite fossils.

activities.text: The descent into the heart of Robin Hood's Bay is worth experiencing properly: lanes so narrow your shoulders brush the walls, cottages built one on top of another into the cliff, the tiny harbour with its cobles (traditional fishing boats). If the tide's out, the exposed rocks on the beach reveal ammonites and belemnites—with good shoes, it's free fossil hunting.

Woodland coastal walk from Hayburn Wyke Inn car park to the waterfall and beach. A 5-kilometre round trip through ancient woods with, as reward, an almost always deserted pebble beach. Bring waterproof footwear.

Whitby harbour at day's end, when the fishing boats return and gulls wheel above the quays, is worth every photo of the trip. Look for pubs and fish & chip shops on both banks of the Esk—queues outside the good places will guide you better than any guidebook.

days_plan.itin_title: Yorkshire's Most Beautiful Places
North York Moors Coast Road Trip: Cliffs and Fishing Villages
Yorkshire and North East England: Dales, Castles and Coastal Drama
Yorkshire and North East England: Dales, Castles and Coastal Drama
Yorkshire: Wild Coast & Secret Moorlands

visits.text: Whitstable is one of the most charming addresses on the Kent coast. The town owes its worldwide reputation to its native oysters, cultivated here since Roman times—in 80 AD, the Romans were already shipping them to Rome. In 1793, the Whitstable Oyster Fishery Company was established by Act of Parliament to regulate fishing, and at its peak, 60 million oysters per year made their way to London. Start at the still-active harbour, with its brightly colored fishing boats. If you're there on a Sunday, the Harbour Market offers local crafts, ceramics, and fresh seafood. Arrive early for the best oysters.

Walking up to Tankerton brings you to Whitstable Castle, a neo-Gothic house built in 1790 for the Pearson family—despite its name, it's not a medieval castle but an elegant private residence converted into a cultural space. The gardens are free to access and worth exploring; the orangery tearoom is perfect for a break. Next door, Tankerton Slopes offer lovely views of the pebble beach and its brightly painted fishermen's huts.

Pop into the Whitstable Museum and Gallery: it houses the Invicta locomotive, one of the world's oldest, a survivor from the Crab and Winkle line—England's first passenger railway, inaugurated in 1830 between Whitstable and Canterbury. The museum also traces the history of oyster fishing and dedicates a section to Peter Cushing, the legendary horror actor who lived in Whitstable for nearly forty years.

activities.text: An oyster lunch at the harbour is the essential ritual. Whitstable produces both native oysters (available only in months with an R, from September to April) and rock oysters available year-round. Several spots line the East Quay: choose a waterfront table and order a dozen with buttered bread.

Walk along the Saxon Shore Way to Tankerton Beach: a peaceful stroll over pebbles with, at low tide, the Street—a ridge of pebbles extending several hundred meters into the sea. On certain days, you can walk knee-deep in water. Sunsets from here are among the most beautiful on Kent's north coast.

days_plan.itin_title: Southeast England & Kent Circuit: White Cliffs and Secret Gardens

visits.text: Ferry back from Stromness to Scrabster, then south along the A99 toward Wick. John O'Groats is worth stopping at for the sign and the view across to the Orkney islands you've just come from - though be clear that it is not the most northerly point of mainland Scotland (that is Dunnet Head, 20 km to the west). The most interesting stop near here is the ruin of Castle Sinclair Girnigoe, on a cliff promontory 5 km north of Wick: a 15th-century fortress that partly collapsed into the sea, its walls split by geological fault lines. Walk from the car park and allow 45 minutes.

The Whaligoe Steps, south of Wick on the A99, descend 365 hand-cut flagstone steps down a sheer cliff to a tiny natural harbour barely large enough for three boats. It was built in the 18th century for the herring fishery - fisherwomen carried full creels of fish up these steps to the road above. The views from the top and bottom are extraordinary. The steps are steep and uneven; allow 30 minutes.

Wick was the herring capital of the world in the 19th century, processing millions of barrels a year for export to the Baltic states and the West Indies. The Wick Heritage Museum has one of the best collections of Victorian industrial photography in Scotland - nearly 70,000 glass plates taken by Johnston & Sons between 1863 and 1977 documenting the town, the fleet and the fishery. Entry is free and the museum is run entirely by volunteers. Allow 1 hour.

activities.text: The Flow Country, west of Wick and accessible via the A882 through Halkirk, is the largest blanket peat bog in Europe - 4,000 square kilometres of undulating peatland that stores more carbon per square metre than tropical rainforest. In 2023 it became a UNESCO World Heritage Site - the first peatland in the world to receive that designation, recognised not just as a landscape but as a critical carbon store whose protection matters to the entire planet. The Forsinard Flows visitor centre, operated by the RSPB, has a walkway over the bog, observation tower and guided tours in season. A surreal, silent landscape unlike anywhere else in Britain.

days_plan.itin_title: North Coast 500 with Orkney: Scotland's Ultimate Coastal Circuit

places.name: Winchester

visits.text: Hampton Court Palace, 20km southwest of London on the Thames, is one of England's best-preserved palaces. Henry VIII resided here from 1525 and installed several of his wives. The Tudor kitchens, capable of feeding 600 people daily, have been reconstructed to their 16th-century state. The formal gardens, designed for William III in the late 17th century, contain England's most famous hedge maze. Allow at least 2.5 hours.

Winchester Cathedral is the world's longest medieval cathedral: 170 meters long, built between 1079 and 1532. Inside, the perpendicular Gothic nave impresses with its height. Jane Austen is buried in the main nave. The bones of Saxon kings of Wessex, including Cnut the Viking, rest in gilded mortuary chests on the choir gallery. Don't miss diver William Walker: between 1906 and 1911, this man worked alone underwater to shore up the foundations with 83,000 bags of concrete and 115,000 blocks – the cathedral was sinking.

In Winchester Castle's Great Hall hangs the famous Round Table. This oak disc measuring 5.5 meters in diameter and weighing 1.2 tons was crafted around 1290 for King Edward I, probably for a tournament celebrating his daughter's betrothal. Henry VIII had it painted in 1522 depicting King Arthur with his own features. It's not Arthur's real table – but the object itself is 700 years old.

activities.text: Walk along the River Itchen from Winchester's center. This crystalline waterway, one of England's most renowned trout rivers, flows past medieval mills. Winchester City Mill, rebuilt in 1744 on a mill site dating back to the Domesday Book (1086), remains operational – you can buy flour milled on site.

days_plan.itin_title: England Grand Tour: From Cornwall to the Northern Lakes

map_keywords: Winchester Castle Great Hall Round Table

places.name: Windermere

visits.text: Welcome to the Lake District - UNESCO-listed since 2017, but this landscape has been changing people for far longer. This is where the English Romantics invented, in the 18th century, the very idea of going to the mountains for pleasure. Stop first at Ambleside to get your bearings and kit up if needed: it's the gateway to the park and outdoor shops are plentiful. Spot the Bridge House as you pass - a tiny 17th-century house perched on a bridge over Stock Ghyll, so narrow you wonder how a family could have lived there.

Dove Cottage in Grasmere is where it all began for English Romantic poetry. Wordsworth and his sister Dorothy lived here from 1799 to 1808 - eight years during which he wrote I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud, inspired by an entry in his sister's journal describing daffodils seen near Ullswater. The building was previously an inn, the Dove and Olive, open since 1617 - which explains the low ceilings and thick walls. Wordsworth paid £8 annual rent and received Coleridge, Walter Scott and Thomas de Quincey. The visit is guided and in small groups: book online ahead, slots fill fast in summer.

Take an hour to stroll around Grasmere: St Oswald's church, where Wordsworth attended Sunday service and where he's buried in the churchyard, creates a particular atmosphere. Grasmere lake a stone's throw away, with its rowing boats for hire at the north end, is a good spot to decompress after visiting Dove Cottage.

Hill Top at Near Sawrey goes far beyond Peter Rabbit nostalgia. Beatrix Potter bought this farmhouse in 1905 with her book royalties, then transformed herself into a fierce Herdwick sheep breeder and Lake District conservation campaigner. When she died in 1943, she bequeathed to the National Trust 4,000 acres and 15 farms on condition they remain worked according to traditional methods - it's largely thanks to her that the park still looks as it does. The house has remained almost unchanged since 1943. Book your slot online ahead - it's mandatory in high season and places go quickly.

Hawkshead is the Lake District's best-preserved medieval village - its cobbled lanes and whitewashed 16th-century houses haven't been disfigured by modern rebuilding. This is where the young Wordsworth studied at the Grammar School between 1779 and 1787; his desk, carved with his name, is still visible. Push open the door to the Beatrix Potter Gallery - housed in the former office of the solicitor she married, it displays original illustrations from her books. Hawkshead is quieter than Windermere and deserves lingering.

activities.text: The hike around Loughrigg Fell from Grasmere is one of the park's most accessible: 8km, 200 metres elevation gain, with views over Grasmere and Rydal Water. Allow 2h30 at an easy pace. The path is waymarked and walkable year-round, but waterproof boots remain essential.

Grasmere Gingerbread is a unique specialty: half-biscuit half-cake, spiced, sold since 1854 from the same shop beside the church. The original recipe is kept in a safe at the local bank. Join the queue - it's worth it.

The ascent of Catbells from Keswick Landing Stage or Hawse End is one of the Lake District's most popular hikes: 451 metres elevation gain, a narrow ridge with 360° views over Derwentwater, Borrowdale and the surrounding fells. Alfred Wainwright described it as 'a hill for all'. Allow 3h with photo stops. The descent can be made on the Newlands Valley side to vary the route.

A cruise on Lake Windermere - England's largest natural lake at 17km long - is the most contemplative way to end the day. Boats run back and forth between Ambleside, Bowness and Lakeside. Take the last departure in the evening for the light.

map_keywords: Hill Top Near Sawrey Lake District

places.name: Wroxham

visits.text: Before you take out a boat, pause to understand what you're about to cross—the Norfolk Broads are nothing natural. What you see is the result of thousands of peat pits dug by hand in the Middle Ages to supply Norwich with fuel, flooded in the fourteenth century when sea levels rose. Nobody understood this until 1952, when botanist Joyce Lambert established the man-made origin of the site after systematic surveys of the banks. The result of this historical accident: 200 kilometres of navigable waterways, 63 lakes and seven rivers linked together, and a national park that shelters a quarter of Britain's rarest species—including the only British habitat of the swallowtail butterfly (Papilio machaon brittanicus).

St Helen's Church in Ranworth is nicknamed the 'cathedral of the Broads': perched on a slight rise at the edge of Ranworth Broad, its fifteenth-century tower is visible from the water several kilometres away. Climb to the top of the tower (89 steps) for the most complete view of the Broads. Inside, the church preserves one of England's finest medieval rood screens, dating from 1450, with its polychrome paintings remarkably well preserved. Ranworth Broad Nature Reserve next door has a floating centre on stilts with bird observation facilities.

The ruins of St Benet's Abbey, accessible only by boat or on foot from the bank, constitute the only Broads site visited each year by the Bishop of Norwich during a ceremony on a boat in August. Founded in 1020 by King Cnut (the same who founded Bury St Edmunds Abbey), it is the only English abbey that was never formally dissolved by Henry VIII—the Bishop of Norwich remains theoretically its abbot. An eighteenth-century windmill was added within the ruins themselves, creating a singular silhouette in the Broads landscape.

Wroxham is your base for taking to the water—it's the 'capital of the Broads' and the first pleasure boat hire company opened here in the 1870s. Today, hundreds of boats are available by the day or by the week, with no locks on the route. If you have children, or even if you don't, pop into Roys of Wroxham: nicknamed 'the world's largest village store', this institution since 1895 occupies several buildings in the centre and sells pretty much everything.

activities.text: Hire a motor boat for a half-day on the Broads from Wroxham: no licence is required and a few minutes' instruction is enough. Follow the River Bure towards Horning or head upstream to Barton Broad, the second-largest lake in the Broads. The calm of the waterways—6 km/h speed limit on most stretches—is striking. In summer, book ahead.

days_plan.itin_title: East Anglia Road Trip: Norfolk, Suffolk and Cambridge
From Cambridge to London: Eastern England in 10 Days

map_keywords: St Benet's Abbey ruins Norfolk Broads

visits.text: York Minster stops you dead as you enter the city - it dominates everything. It's northern Europe's largest medieval cathedral, and its construction lasted 252 years (1220-1472). The first church on this site was built in wood in 627 for the baptism of King Edwin of Northumbria. Inside, look up: 128 medieval stained glass windows, the UK's largest collection - including the Great East Window, surface-equivalent to a tennis court. In 1984, lightning cracked the Rose Window into 40,000 pieces; restoration traces are visible if you look closely. Climb the 275 steps of the central tower - the view over York from up there is the city's best. Allow 2h minimum.

The Shambles is mentioned in the Domesday Book of 1086 - back then, butchers hung meat from hooks still visible on some facades. The overhanging upper storeys lean so close from one side to the other you could almost shake hands from window to window. The street is often cited as the inspiration for Diagon Alley in Harry Potter, which J.K. Rowling has never confirmed or denied. Avoid it between 11am and 4pm in summer - the flow is relentless; early morning or evening, it's a different atmosphere.

The National Railway Museum is the world's largest railway museum - and it's free. Look first for Mallard: this locomotive set the world speed record for steam in 1938, 202.8 km/h, a record that still stands. Move on to Queen Victoria's and Edward VII's royal silk-and-wood carriages - the gap between royal comfort and third-class carriages in the same era is striking. A 1960s Japanese Shinkansen sits alongside a Eurostar in the same shed: 150 years of engineering in a single glance.

York Castle Museum occupies the city's former prisons, including the one where Dick Turpin, the famous highwayman, awaited his execution in 1739. Inside, you'll find a fully reconstructed Victorian street - Kirkgate - with its shops, blacksmith and horse-drawn omnibus. This isn't a dusty museum: the reconstruction is dense enough to linger in. The 20th-century toy room is northern England's most nostalgic collection - allow an hour and a half.

York's Chocolate Story answers a question everyone should ask: how did a provincial Yorkshire town become the capital of British chocolate? Terry's opened here in 1767 - the first shop was near Bootham Bar - and Rowntree's followed in 1862, inventing the KitKat, Smarties and Aero. The guided tour takes you from the ritual origins of cocoa in Central America to Yorkshire production lines, with tasting included. Book online - tickets are cheaper there and there may be a wait in high season.

activities.text: The Jorvik Viking Centre is installed exactly where a Viking quarter was excavated in the 1970s - 1,000 years of organic waste perfectly preserved in clay, including shoes, combs, food and even intestinal parasites. You board a cart that transports you to the archaeological level, with sound and smell reconstruction - yes, smells are included. Book online: without booking, the queue can exceed an hour, and it's for about 45 minutes of visit.

York's walls are England's best-preserved: 3.4km continuous and freely accessible, built in the 13th-14th centuries on Roman and Viking foundations. The stretch between Bootham Bar and Monk Bar is best - direct views of York Minster, gardens below, medieval lanes visible from the battlements. Allow 1h30 for the full circuit, less if you only do the northern section.

Don't laugh: York is officially England's most haunted city according to the Society for Psychical Research - it's not marketing. Walking Ghost Tours run every evening through the historic centre's lanes. The best-known stop is the Treasurer's House, where a plumber claimed in 1953 to have seen a legion of Roman soldiers marching through the basement - at mid-leg height, as if following a Roman road buried since. Archaeological investigation did indeed find Roman paving at that depth. Departures generally at 8pm, allow 1h30.

To finish the evening gently, descend to the Ouse from Lendal Bridge and walk to Skeldergate Bridge - 2km round trip along the riverbanks. The riverside pub terraces are the right addresses for a last pint before heading back.
